explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why the oxy-hydrogen flame can be used in welding.
explanation.principle: Combustion of hydrogen in oxygen gives a very high temperature flame.
steps: Hydrogen burns vigorously in oxygen.
A large amount of heat is released.
The flame temperature is high enough for welding in some applications.
Therefore the flame is useful because it is very hot.
explanation.simple_explanation: The oxy-hydrogen flame is valuable because of its high temperature. Check our "Tutorial section" for uses of hydrogen.

question: When hydrogen burns, the product can be recognized by the formation of

options.A: water droplets on a cool surface

answer_text: water droplets on a cool surface

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify evidence that hydrogen combustion forms water.
explanation.principle: Water vapour produced can condense on a cool surface.
steps: Hydrogen reacts with oxygen to form water.
If the vapour touches a cool surface, droplets may appear.
This supports the idea that water is formed.
Therefore water droplets are the correct observation.
explanation.final_answer: A. water droplets on a cool surface
explanation.simple_explanation: Hydrogen burning gives water, which may condense as droplets. Check our "Tutorial section" for combustion observations.

subtopic: Hydrogen: commercial production from water gas and petroleum cracking
question: Which gas is removed after the water-gas shift reaction in order to obtain a purer hydrogen stream?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the gas commonly removed after the shift reaction.
explanation.principle: The shift reaction produces CO₂ and H₂ from CO and steam.
steps: After the shift step, the gas mixture contains hydrogen and carbon(IV) oxide.
Hydrogen is the desired product.
So the carbon(IV) oxide is removed to purify the hydrogen.
Therefore CO₂ is the gas removed.
explanation.simple_explanation: Removing CO₂ helps purify hydrogen after the shift reaction. Check our "Tutorial section" for industrial separation ideas.

question: Which equation correctly shows magnesium reacting with hydrochloric acid?

explanation.question_restate: We are to select the balanced equation for the reaction of magnesium with hydrochloric acid.
explanation.principle: Magnesium forms Mg²⁺ and the acid supplies chloride ions and hydrogen ions.
steps: Magnesium needs two chloride ions to form MgCl₂.
Two hydrogen ions combine to make H₂.
So the balanced equation uses two HCl molecules.
Therefore option B is correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: Balancing helps you see both the salt and hydrogen clearly. Check our "Tutorial section" for worked equations.

question: Why is helium often preferred to hydrogen in balloons even though hydrogen gives better lift?

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why helium is often chosen over hydrogen in balloons.
explanation.principle: Safety is a major factor in practical applications of gases.
steps: Hydrogen is highly flammable.
Helium is chemically inert and does not burn.
Although hydrogen may lift better, helium is safer.
Therefore helium is preferred because it is non-flammable.
explanation.simple_explanation: Safety often determines which gas is used in practice. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas comparisons.

question: Chlorine belongs to Group 17 because it has

explanation.question_restate: We are to relate chlorine’s group to its outer-shell electrons.
explanation.principle: Main-group elements are often placed by the number of valence electrons.
steps: Chlorine has seven electrons in its outermost shell.
This is typical of the halogens.
Halogens therefore tend to gain one electron in simple ionic compounds.
So chlorine belongs to Group 17.
explanation.simple_explanation: Group 17 elements have seven valence electrons. Check our "Tutorial section" for periodicity.

question: Which statement about chlorine as a representative halogen is correct?

options.A: it forms Cl⁻ by gaining one electron
options.D: it has one electron in the outer shell

answer_text: it forms Cl⁻ by gaining one electron

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose the statement that shows chlorine behaving like a typical halogen.
explanation.principle: Halogens tend to gain one electron to complete their octet.
steps: Chlorine needs one more electron to complete its outer shell.
So it readily forms chloride ion, Cl⁻, in many compounds.
This behaviour is typical of Group 17.
Therefore option A is correct.
explanation.final_answer: A. it forms Cl⁻ by gaining one electron
explanation.simple_explanation: Chlorine behaves like a typical halogen by forming Cl⁻. Check our "Tutorial section" for group trends.

question: In the laboratory, chlorine is prepared by heating manganese(IV) oxide with concentrated

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the acid used in preparing chlorine with MnO₂.
explanation.principle: Concentrated hydrochloric acid supplies chloride ions that are oxidized to chlorine.
steps: MnO₂ reacts with concentrated HCl.
The products include Cl₂, water and manganese(II) chloride.
This is a standard laboratory preparation.
Therefore HCl is correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: Concentrated HCl is used with MnO₂ to prepare chlorine. Check our "Tutorial section" for lab preparations.

question: Why is chlorine not usually collected over water?

options.B: it is soluble in water and reacts with it
options.D: it cannot be produced as a gas

answer_text: it is soluble in water and reacts with it

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why water is unsuitable for collecting chlorine.
explanation.principle: A gas that dissolves in water or reacts with it should not be collected by water displacement.
steps: Chlorine dissolves in water.
It also reacts with water to form acidic and oxidizing products.
So much of the gas would be lost if water were used.
Therefore chlorine is not collected over water.
explanation.final_answer: B. it is soluble in water and reacts with it
explanation.simple_explanation: Do not collect gases over water if they dissolve or react in it. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas collection methods.

question: The raw material used for industrial manufacture of chlorine is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the industrial source of chlorine.
explanation.principle: Concentrated sodium chloride solution is called brine.
steps: Brine is electrolyzed in industrial cells.
Chlorine is produced at the anode.
Hydrogen and sodium hydroxide are also formed.
Therefore brine is the raw material used.
explanation.simple_explanation: Industrial chlorine comes from electrolysis of brine. Check our "Tutorial section" for electrolysis.

question: In brine electrolysis, hydrogen is liberated at the

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the electrode where hydrogen appears during brine electrolysis.
explanation.principle: Reduction takes place at the cathode.
steps: At the cathode, water is reduced in the aqueous system.
This produces hydrogen gas.
At the anode, chloride ions give chlorine gas.
So hydrogen is liberated at the cathode.
explanation.simple_explanation: Remember: chlorine at the anode, hydrogen at the cathode. Check our "Tutorial section" for electrode products.

question: One common use of chlorine is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a familiar use of chlorine.
explanation.principle: Chlorine and its dissolved products destroy many microorganisms.
steps: Small amounts of chlorine compounds can disinfect water.
This helps make drinking water safer.
The effect depends on chlorine’s oxidizing action.
Therefore sterilizing water is a common use.
explanation.simple_explanation: Chlorine is widely used in water treatment. Check our "Tutorial section" for practical uses.

question: The bleaching action of chlorine is mainly due to

options.B: oxidation in the presence of moisture

answer_text: oxidation in the presence of moisture

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain how chlorine bleaches coloured materials.
explanation.principle: Moist chlorine produces active oxidizing species.
steps: Chlorine alone is not very effective when dry.
In the presence of moisture, chlorine forms substances that oxidize colouring matter.
The coloured substance is changed to a colourless product.
So the bleaching action is mainly due to oxidation in moist conditions.
explanation.final_answer: B. oxidation in the presence of moisture
explanation.simple_explanation: Chlorine bleaches by oxidation when moisture is present. Check our "Tutorial section" for bleaching agents.

subtopic: Halogens: hydrogen chloride and hydrochloric acid
question: Hydrogen chloride becomes hydrochloric acid when it

explanation.question_restate: We are to distinguish between HCl gas and hydrochloric acid.
explanation.principle: The name hydrochloric acid refers to the aqueous solution of hydrogen chloride.
steps: Hydrogen chloride in the gas state is HCl gas.
When it dissolves in water, it ionizes and forms an acidic solution.
That solution is called hydrochloric acid.
Therefore dissolution in water is the key step.
explanation.simple_explanation: HCl gas becomes hydrochloric acid in water. Check our "Tutorial section" for acids and ionization.

subtopic: Halogens: hydrogen chloride and hydrochloric acid
question: Why does dry hydrogen chloride not show acidic behavior with dry litmus paper?

options.B: it does not ionize in the absence of water

answer_text: it does not ionize in the absence of water

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why dry HCl gas does not act like an acid on dry litmus.
explanation.principle: The normal acid reaction with indicators depends on ions in water.
steps: Dry hydrogen chloride is covalent.
Without water, the ions responsible for acidity are not produced in the ordinary indicator test.
So dry litmus shows no usual acid change.
Therefore lack of ionization in the absence of water is the reason.
explanation.final_answer: B. it does not ionize in the absence of water
explanation.simple_explanation: Water is needed for the usual litmus acid test. Check our "Tutorial section" for indicator behavior.

subtopic: Halogens: chlorides and test for chlorides
question: A white precipitate with acidified AgNO₃ suggests the presence of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the ion indicated by the white precipitate in the silver nitrate test.
explanation.principle: Silver nitrate gives a white precipitate of AgCl with chloride ions.
steps: The sample is first acidified to remove some interfering ions.
When AgNO₃ is added, chloride ions give AgCl.
AgCl is white and insoluble in water.
So the ion indicated is chloride.
explanation.simple_explanation: A white AgCl precipitate points to chloride ions. Check our "Tutorial section" for qualitative analysis.

subtopic: Halogens: chlorides and test for chlorides
question: The white precipitate obtained in the chloride test is

explanation.question_restate: We are to name the white precipitate formed in the chloride test.
explanation.principle: Silver ions combine with chloride ions to form silver chloride.
steps: Ag⁺ comes from silver nitrate.
Cl⁻ comes from the sample.
The insoluble product is AgCl.
That white solid is silver chloride.
explanation.simple_explanation: Silver chloride is the white precipitate in the chloride test. Check our "Tutorial section" for precipitate colors.

question: Chlorine water is pale in colour because it contains

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why chlorine water has a pale colour.
explanation.principle: The colour comes from chlorine dissolved in water.
steps: Chlorine gas has a greenish-yellow colour.
When a small amount dissolves in water, the solution becomes pale yellow-green.
The intensity is lower because the chlorine is diluted.
Therefore dissolved chlorine is responsible.
explanation.simple_explanation: Chlorine water owes its pale colour to dissolved chlorine. Check our "Tutorial section" for common observations.

question: Chlorine water becomes less coloured on standing in sunlight mainly because

options.A: the chlorine is destroyed in light-driven reactions
options.C: silver chloride forms in all cases

answer_text: the chlorine is destroyed in light-driven reactions

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why chlorine water fades in sunlight.
explanation.principle: Chlorine water is unstable and light promotes reactions that reduce dissolved chlorine.
steps: The dissolved chlorine and products formed with water are not completely stable.
Sunlight helps decomposition reactions to occur.
The amount of dissolved chlorine falls, so the colour fades.
Therefore light-driven destruction of chlorine explains the change.
explanation.final_answer: A. the chlorine is destroyed in light-driven reactions
explanation.simple_explanation: Chlorine water fades because it is unstable in light. Check our "Tutorial section" for practical observations.

subtopic: Halogens: hydrogen chloride and hydrochloric acid
question: Which gas forms dense white fumes when brought near ammonia gas?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the gas that forms dense white fumes with ammonia.
explanation.principle: HCl reacts with NH₃ to form solid NH₄Cl.
steps: Ammonia gas is NH₃.
Hydrogen chloride gas is HCl.
They combine to give ammonium chloride, NH₄Cl.
The fine solid appears as dense white fumes.
explanation.simple_explanation: HCl gives dense white fumes with ammonia because NH₄Cl forms. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas identification.

question: A membrane in the chlor-alkali process helps mainly to

options.B: keep chlorine from mixing with hydrogen and alkali
options.C: turn NaCl into metal sodium directly

answer_text: keep chlorine from mixing with hydrogen and alkali

explanation.question_restate: We are to state the function of the membrane in brine electrolysis.
explanation.principle: Separating products improves safety and purity.
steps: Chlorine forms at one electrode and hydrogen at the other.
Sodium hydroxide also accumulates in solution.
The membrane helps prevent these products from mixing.
Therefore it is used to keep chlorine separate from hydrogen and alkali.
explanation.final_answer: B. keep chlorine from mixing with hydrogen and alkali
explanation.simple_explanation: Industrial electrolytic cells separate products for safety and purity. Check our "Tutorial section" for the chlor-alkali process.

question: Which of the following is a chloride?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a compound containing chloride ions.
explanation.principle: A chloride is a compound containing Cl⁻ as the negative ion.
steps: NaCl contains sodium ions and chloride ions.
Na₂SO₄ is a sulphate, KNO₃ is a nitrate, and CaCO₃ is a carbonate.
Therefore the chloride is NaCl.
This is a straightforward naming question.
explanation.simple_explanation: A chloride contains Cl⁻ ions. Check our "Tutorial section" for names of salts.

question: The choking smell of chlorine is an example of a

explanation.question_restate: We are to classify the smell of chlorine.
explanation.principle: A physical property is observed without changing the substance chemically.
steps: Smell is something that can be sensed directly.
This does not by itself describe a chemical reaction.
Therefore choking smell is a physical property.
So option A is correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: Smell and color are physical properties. Check our "Tutorial section" for distinguishing physical and chemical properties.

subtopic: Halogens: chlorides and test for chlorides
question: Why is dilute nitric acid used before adding AgNO₃ in the chloride test?

options.B: to remove interfering ions such as carbonates

answer_text: to remove interfering ions such as carbonates

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the acidification step in the chloride test.
explanation.principle: Some anions could otherwise give misleading precipitates with silver ions.
steps: Carbonate ions can also react with silver ions.
Adding dilute nitric acid removes such interference.
Nitric acid is used because nitrate ions do not interfere with the test.
Therefore the purpose is to remove interfering ions.
explanation.final_answer: B. to remove interfering ions such as carbonates
explanation.simple_explanation: Acidification helps avoid false positives. Check our "Tutorial section" for qualitative analysis.

subtopic: Halogens: hydrogen chloride and hydrochloric acid
question: Which statement best distinguishes chlorine gas from hydrogen chloride gas?

options.A: chlorine bleaches moist litmus, while hydrogen chloride forms white fumes with ammonia
options.B: both gases are colourless and support combustion
options.D: hydrogen chloride relights a glowing splint

answer_text: chlorine bleaches moist litmus, while hydrogen chloride forms white fumes with ammonia

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose a pair of observations that separates Cl₂ from HCl.
explanation.principle: Different gases have different characteristic tests.
steps: Chlorine bleaches moist litmus due to its oxidizing action.
Hydrogen chloride forms dense white fumes with ammonia because NH₄Cl is formed.
These are strong distinguishing tests.
Therefore option A is correct.
explanation.final_answer: A. chlorine bleaches moist litmus, while hydrogen chloride forms white fumes with ammonia
explanation.simple_explanation: Use characteristic gas tests to distinguish gases. Check our "Tutorial section" for comparison tables.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: oxygen: preparation, properties, uses, commercial production from liquid air
question: In the laboratory, oxygen can be prepared by heating potassium trioxochlorate(V) with

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the substance used with KClO₃ in laboratory preparation of oxygen.
explanation.principle: MnO₂ acts as a catalyst for decomposition of KClO₃.
steps: KClO₃ decomposes on heating to give KCl and O₂.
MnO₂ speeds the decomposition.
It is a common laboratory method for oxygen preparation.
Therefore manganese(IV) oxide is correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: KClO₃ with MnO₂ is a standard lab source of oxygen. Check our "Tutorial section" for preparation methods.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: oxygen: preparation, properties, uses, commercial production from liquid air
question: Commercial oxygen is obtained from liquid air by

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the industrial method for producing oxygen from air.
explanation.principle: The components of liquid air have different boiling points and can be separated by distillation.
steps: Air is first liquefied.
The liquid mixture is then fractionally distilled.
Nitrogen and oxygen separate because of their different boiling points.
Therefore oxygen is obtained by fractional distillation of liquid air.
explanation.simple_explanation: Industrial oxygen comes from liquid air by fractional distillation. Check our "Tutorial section" for separation methods.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: oxygen: preparation, properties, uses, commercial production from liquid air
question: The standard test for oxygen is that it

options.D: forms dense white fumes with ammonia

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the common test for oxygen.
explanation.principle: Oxygen supports combustion strongly.
steps: A glowing splint is only partly burning.
When introduced into oxygen, it bursts into flame again.
This is called rekindling.
Therefore oxygen relights a glowing splint.
explanation.simple_explanation: Oxygen is recognized because it relights a glowing splint. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas tests.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: oxides: acidic, basic, amphoteric and neutral
question: Which oxide is basic?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a basic oxide.
explanation.principle: Basic oxides are often metallic oxides, especially from reactive metals.
steps: Na₂O is an oxide of a reactive metal.
It reacts with water to give an alkaline solution.
CO₂ and SO₂ are acidic oxides, while Al₂O₃ is amphoteric.
Therefore Na₂O is basic.
explanation.simple_explanation: Many metal oxides are basic. Check our "Tutorial section" for oxide classification.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: oxides: acidic, basic, amphoteric and neutral
question: Which oxide is amphoteric?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify an amphoteric oxide.
explanation.principle: Amphoteric oxides react with both acids and alkalis.
steps: Al₂O₃ reacts with acids and with strong bases.
SO₃ is acidic, CO is neutral, and MgO is basic.
Therefore Al₂O₃ is the amphoteric oxide.
This is a classic JAMB-style classification item.
explanation.simple_explanation: Al₂O₃ is a standard example of an amphoteric oxide. Check our "Tutorial section" for oxide types.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: ozone as an allotrope and its atmospheric importance
question: Ozone is an allotrope of oxygen with formula

explanation.question_restate: We are to state the formula of ozone.
explanation.principle: Ozone is another molecular form of oxygen.
steps: Ordinary oxygen is O₂.
Ozone contains three oxygen atoms in each molecule.
So its formula is O₃.
Therefore option C is correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: Ozone is O₃, an allotrope of oxygen. Check our "Tutorial section" for allotropy.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: ozone as an allotrope and its atmospheric importance
question: The main atmospheric importance of ozone is that it

explanation.question_restate: We are to state why ozone high in the atmosphere is important.
explanation.principle: The ozone layer protects living things from much ultraviolet radiation.
steps: Ozone in the upper atmosphere absorbs significant UV radiation.
This reduces the amount reaching Earth’s surface.
Without this shield, living organisms would be more exposed to damage.
Therefore absorption of harmful UV is its main importance.
explanation.simple_explanation: The ozone layer protects Earth by absorbing UV radiation. Check our "Tutorial section" for atmospheric chemistry.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: sulphur: allotropes and uses
question: Two common crystalline allotropes of sulphur are

explanation.question_restate: We are to name the common crystalline allotropes of sulphur.
explanation.principle: Sulphur exists in more than one structural form.
steps: Rhombic sulphur is stable at room temperature.
Monoclinic sulphur is stable at higher temperatures.
Both are forms of the same element, sulphur.
Therefore the pair is rhombic and monoclinic sulphur.
explanation.simple_explanation: Rhombic and monoclinic are the common crystalline allotropes of sulphur. Check our "Tutorial section" for allotropes.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: sulphur: allotropes and uses
question: A major industrial use of sulphur is in the manufacture of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a major industrial use of sulphur.
explanation.principle: Sulphur is an important raw material for sulphuric acid production.
steps: Sulphur is burned to form SO₂.
SO₂ is processed further in the contact process.
This leads to H₂SO₄ manufacture.
Therefore sulphur is widely used in making tetraoxosulphate(VI) acid.
explanation.simple_explanation: Sulphur is vital in H₂SO₄ production. Check our "Tutorial section" for the contact process.

question: Sulphur(IV) oxide is formed when sulphur burns in

explanation.question_restate: We are to state how SO₂ is formed from sulphur.
explanation.principle: Combustion means burning in oxygen.
steps: Sulphur burns with a blue flame.
Its main product in oxygen is sulphur(IV) oxide, SO₂.
This gas has a choking smell.
Therefore oxygen is the correct answer.
explanation.simple_explanation: Burning sulphur in oxygen gives SO₂. Check our "Tutorial section" for sulphur compounds.

question: Sulphur(IV) oxide is often described as a reducing agent because it can

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a reaction that shows SO₂ acting as a reducing agent.
explanation.principle: Reducing agents can remove color from strong oxidizing agents such as acidified permanganate.
steps: Acidified KMnO₄ is purple.
SO₂ can reduce it and cause the purple color to disappear.
This shows the reducing property of SO₂.
Therefore option A is correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: SO₂ behaves as a reducing agent in redox tests. Check our "Tutorial section" for bleaching and redox.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: trioxosulphate(IV) acid and salts
question: The salts of trioxosulphate(IV) acid are known as

explanation.question_restate: We are to name the salts formed from H₂SO₃.
explanation.principle: The acid H₂SO₃ gives salts containing the sulphite ion.
steps: Trioxosulphate(IV) acid is H₂SO₃.
Its salts contain SO₃²⁻.
These salts are called sulphites.
Therefore option B is correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: Sulphites come from sulphur(IV) acid. Check our "Tutorial section" for names of salts.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: tetraoxosulphate(VI) acid, including contact process
question: The catalyst used in the contact process is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the catalyst used in the contact process.
explanation.principle: V₂O₅ is the standard catalyst for oxidation of SO₂ to SO₃.
steps: SO₂ is oxidized to SO₃ in the contact process.
The reaction is speeded up by V₂O₅.
This catalyst helps equilibrium be reached more quickly.
Therefore vanadium(V) oxide is correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: V₂O₅ is the contact-process catalyst. Check our "Tutorial section" for industrial processes.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: tetraoxosulphate(VI) acid, including contact process
question: Why is SO₃ not absorbed directly in water during the contact process?

options.A: it is completely insoluble in water
options.B: it forms acid mist and reacts violently

answer_text: it forms acid mist and reacts violently

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why the contact process does not absorb SO₃ directly in water.
explanation.principle: Direct absorption is difficult to control and gives problematic acid mist.
steps: SO₃ reacts very violently with water.
The product forms a mist that is hard to condense efficiently.
So SO₃ is first absorbed in concentrated H₂SO₄ to form oleum.
Therefore violent reaction and acid mist are the reasons.
explanation.final_answer: B. it forms acid mist and reacts violently
explanation.simple_explanation: The contact process avoids direct absorption of SO₃ in water. Check our "Tutorial section" for the process flow.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: test for sulphate
question: Sulphate ions are tested for using acidified

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the reagent used in the sulphate test.
explanation.principle: Barium ions give an insoluble white precipitate with sulphate ions.
steps: The solution is first acidified to remove some interference.
Barium chloride is then added.
If sulphate ions are present, white BaSO₄ appears.
Therefore acidified barium chloride is used.
explanation.simple_explanation: Acidified BaCl₂ is the standard sulphate test. Check our "Tutorial section" for anion tests.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: hydrogen sulphide: preparation, properties and test for sulphide
question: Hydrogen sulphide is recognized by its

options.D: ability to relight a glowing splint

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a familiar property of hydrogen sulphide.
explanation.principle: H₂S has a characteristic offensive smell.
steps: Hydrogen sulphide is a colourless gas.
It has a strong smell like rotten eggs.
This is a common identifying feature, though smell is not the safest test.
Therefore option B is correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: H₂S is well known for its rotten-egg smell. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas properties.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: hydrogen sulphide: preparation, properties and test for sulphide
question: A confirmatory test for hydrogen sulphide is that it turns moist lead(II) ethanoate paper

explanation.question_restate: We are to recall the confirmatory test for H₂S.
explanation.principle: Hydrogen sulphide forms black lead(II) sulphide with lead(II) salts.
steps: Lead(II) ethanoate paper is exposed to the gas.
If H₂S is present, PbS forms.
PbS is black.
So the paper turns black.
explanation.simple_explanation: Blackening of lead(II) salt paper confirms H₂S. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas tests.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: oxygen: preparation, properties, uses, commercial production from liquid air
question: Which statement about oxygen is correct?

options.A: it is a fuel in ordinary combustion
options.B: it supports combustion but is not itself the fuel

answer_text: it supports combustion but is not itself the fuel

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose a correct statement about oxygen.
explanation.principle: Oxygen is the oxidizing medium in many combustion reactions.
steps: When a substance burns, oxygen supports the process.
The burning substance is the fuel, not the oxygen.
This explains why oxygen relights a glowing splint.
Therefore option B is correct.
explanation.final_answer: B. it supports combustion but is not itself the fuel
explanation.simple_explanation: Oxygen supports burning rather than acting as the usual fuel. Check our "Tutorial section" for combustion basics.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: sulphur: allotropes and uses
question: Rhombic sulphur and monoclinic sulphur are regarded as allotropes because they

options.B: contain only sulphur but differ in structure
options.C: always have the same crystal form

answer_text: contain only sulphur but differ in structure

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why rhombic and monoclinic sulphur are allotropes.
explanation.principle: Allotropes are different structural forms of the same element.
steps: Both substances contain only sulphur atoms.
They differ in crystal arrangement and stability conditions.
That structural difference makes them distinct forms of sulphur.
Therefore they are allotropes.
explanation.final_answer: B. contain only sulphur but differ in structure
explanation.simple_explanation: Allotropy means same element, different structure. Check our "Tutorial section" for allotropes.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: tetraoxosulphate(VI) acid, including contact process
question: The overall purpose of the contact process is the manufacture of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the main product of the contact process.
explanation.principle: The contact process is the standard industrial method for producing sulphuric acid.
steps: Sulphur or sulphide ores are used to obtain SO₂.
SO₂ is oxidized to SO₃ in the presence of V₂O₅.
The SO₃ is then absorbed and processed to form sulphuric acid.
Therefore the final industrial product is H₂SO₄.
explanation.simple_explanation: The contact process is the major industrial route to sulphuric acid. Check our "Tutorial section" for named industrial processes.

question: In the laboratory, nitrogen is commonly prepared by warming a mixture that forms ammonium nitrite because ammonium nitrite decomposes to

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the products of decomposition of ammonium nitrite.
explanation.principle: Ammonium nitrite is unstable and decomposes to nitrogen and water.
steps: The compound is prepared in situ because of its instability.
On warming, it breaks down.
The gaseous product is N₂ and water is also formed.
Therefore option B is correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: Ammonium nitrite breaks down to nitrogen and water. Check our "Tutorial section" for lab preparations.

question: Nitrogen is obtained before oxygen in fractional distillation of liquid air because nitrogen has

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why nitrogen separates before oxygen from liquid air.
explanation.principle: The component with lower boiling point comes off more readily.
steps: Nitrogen boils at a lower temperature than oxygen.
So when liquid air is distilled, nitrogen is the more volatile component.
It separates first more readily.
Therefore the lower boiling point is the reason.
explanation.simple_explanation: Fractional distillation depends on boiling-point differences. Check our "Tutorial section" for separation methods.

subtopic: Nitrogen: ammonia: Haber process, properties, uses
question: The raw materials for the Haber process are

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the reactants in the Haber process.
explanation.principle: Ammonia is made by combining nitrogen and hydrogen.
steps: The equation is N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃.
Nitrogen provides the nitrogen atom in NH₃.
Hydrogen provides the hydrogen atoms.
Therefore N₂ and H₂ are the raw materials.
explanation.simple_explanation: The Haber process combines nitrogen and hydrogen. Check our "Tutorial section" for process conditions.

subtopic: Nitrogen: ammonia: Haber process, properties, uses
question: A catalyst used in the Haber process is finely divided

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the catalyst used in the Haber process.
explanation.principle: Iron is the classic industrial catalyst for ammonia manufacture.
steps: The Haber reaction would be too slow without a catalyst.
Finely divided iron speeds up the reaction.
It helps equilibrium be reached faster.
Therefore iron is the correct answer.
explanation.simple_explanation: Iron is the standard catalyst in the Haber process. Check our "Tutorial section" for industrial catalysts.

subtopic: Nitrogen: ammonia: Haber process, properties, uses
question: One property of ammonia is that it

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a characteristic property of ammonia.
explanation.principle: Ammonia behaves as a basic gas in moist conditions.
steps: NH₃ dissolves partly in water to form an alkaline solution.
This alkaline effect turns damp red litmus blue.
The other options belong to other gases.
Therefore option A is correct.
explanation.final_answer: A. turns moist red litmus blue
explanation.simple_explanation: Ammonia is an alkaline gas in moist conditions. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas tests.

subtopic: Nitrogen: ammonia: Haber process, properties, uses
question: A major use of ammonia is in the manufacture of

explanation.question_restate: We are to state a major use of ammonia.
explanation.principle: Ammonia is an important starting material in fertilizer production.
steps: Ammonia can be converted into ammonium salts.
It can also be used to make nitric acid.
These products are key fertilizer materials.
Therefore fertilizers are a major use.
explanation.simple_explanation: Ammonia is crucial in fertilizer manufacture. Check our "Tutorial section" for industrial importance.

question: When an ammonium salt is warmed with sodium hydroxide, the gas evolved is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the gas evolved from an ammonium salt treated with alkali.
explanation.principle: Strong alkalis liberate ammonia from ammonium salts.
steps: The ammonium ion is NH₄⁺.
With NaOH and heat, NH₃ is released.
This is the basis of the test for ammonium ions.
Therefore ammonia is evolved.
explanation.simple_explanation: Warm alkali liberates NH₃ from ammonium salts. Check our "Tutorial section" for cation tests.

subtopic: Nitrogen: oxidation of ammonia to nitrogen(IV) oxide and trioxonitrate(V) acid
question: In the Ostwald process, ammonia is first oxidized to

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the first product in the oxidation of ammonia during nitric acid manufacture.
explanation.principle: The Ostwald process occurs in stages.
steps: Ammonia is first oxidized to nitrogen(II) oxide, NO.
NO is then oxidized further to NO₂.
The oxides then help form nitric acid.
Therefore the first product is NO.
explanation.simple_explanation: The first gaseous product in the Ostwald process is NO. Check our "Tutorial section" for process sequences.

question: Which observation confirms ammonium ions after warming with NaOH?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the confirmatory observation for ammonia from an ammonium salt.
explanation.principle: Ammonia is alkaline and changes damp red litmus to blue.
steps: Warming with NaOH releases ammonia if NH₄⁺ is present.
Ammonia has a pungent smell.
It also turns moist red litmus blue.
Therefore this litmus change confirms ammonium ions.
explanation.final_answer: B. moist red litmus turns blue
explanation.simple_explanation: Ammonia from ammonium salts turns damp red litmus blue. Check our "Tutorial section" for cation analysis.

question: Trioxonitrate(V) acid is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify trioxonitrate(V) acid from its formula.
explanation.principle: This is the systematic name for nitric acid.
steps: The acid is commonly called nitric acid.
Its formula is HNO₃.
Its salts are called nitrates.
Therefore HNO₃ is correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: Nitric acid is trioxonitrate(V) acid. Check our "Tutorial section" for naming practice.

question: A general property of nitrates is that they are

explanation.question_restate: We are to state a common solubility rule for nitrates.
explanation.principle: Many nitrates are soluble, which is a key rule in qualitative chemistry.
steps: Nitrate salts generally dissolve in water.
This contrasts with many sulphates and carbonates.
The rule is very useful in analysis and equation work.
Therefore option B is correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: Nitrates are generally soluble in water. Check our "Tutorial section" for solubility rules.

question: The brown gas among the oxides of nitrogen is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the brown oxide of nitrogen.
explanation.principle: Nitrogen(IV) oxide has a characteristic brown colour.
steps: NO is colourless.
NO₂ is brown.
This color often appears in nitrate decomposition and nitric acid reactions.
Therefore NO₂ is the brown gas.
explanation.simple_explanation: NO₂ is the familiar brown oxide of nitrogen. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas properties.

question: The colourless oxide of nitrogen that turns brown in air is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the gas that is colourless first but becomes brown in air.
explanation.principle: NO is oxidized in air to NO₂, which is brown.
steps: Fresh NO is colourless.
In the presence of oxygen, it changes to NO₂.
The appearance therefore becomes brown.
So the original colourless gas is NO.
explanation.simple_explanation: NO turns into brown NO₂ in air. Check our "Tutorial section" for nitrogen oxides.

question: A process that converts atmospheric nitrogen into useful compounds is called

explanation.question_restate: We are to name the process that makes atmospheric nitrogen useful to living things.
explanation.principle: Free nitrogen gas is very stable and must be converted to combined nitrogen compounds.
steps: This conversion can be done by bacteria, lightning, or industry.
The overall process is called nitrogen fixation.
It makes nitrogen available in forms plants can use.
Therefore option B is correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: Nitrogen fixation makes atmospheric nitrogen usable. Check our "Tutorial section" for the nitrogen cycle.

question: The conversion of ammonium compounds to nitrates in the soil is called

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the soil process that forms nitrates from ammonium compounds.
explanation.principle: Different stages in the nitrogen cycle have different names.
steps: Nitrifying bacteria oxidize ammonium compounds.
This produces nitrites and then nitrates.
The overall process is called nitrification.
Therefore option B is correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: Nitrification turns ammonium compounds into nitrates. Check our "Tutorial section" for cycle terminology.

subtopic: Nitrogen: ammonia: Haber process, properties, uses
question: Why is moderate temperature used in the Haber process rather than extremely high temperature?

options.A: the reaction is exothermic and very high temperature lowers the equilibrium yield

answer_text: the reaction is exothermic and very high temperature lowers the equilibrium yield

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why the Haber process uses moderate rather than extremely high temperature.
explanation.principle: Industrial conditions balance reaction rate with equilibrium yield.
steps: The Haber reaction is exothermic.
Lower temperature favors ammonia yield at equilibrium.
But too low a temperature makes the reaction too slow.
So a moderate temperature is used as a compromise.
explanation.final_answer: A. the reaction is exothermic and very high temperature lowers the equilibrium yield
explanation.simple_explanation: The Haber process uses compromise conditions for both rate and yield. Check our "Tutorial section" for process reasoning.

subtopic: Nitrogen: ammonia: Haber process, properties, uses
question: Why is high pressure used in the Haber process?

options.A: to favor the side with fewer gas molecules
options.C: to stop the catalyst from working

answer_text: to favor the side with fewer gas molecules

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the choice of high pressure in ammonia manufacture.
explanation.principle: High pressure favors the equilibrium side with fewer moles of gas.
steps: N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃ shows four gas moles on the left and two on the right.
Increasing pressure favors the side with fewer gas molecules.
This increases ammonia yield.
Therefore option A is correct.
explanation.final_answer: A. to favor the side with fewer gas molecules
explanation.simple_explanation: Pressure favors the side with fewer gas molecules. Check our "Tutorial section" for equilibrium.

question: Which compound is an ammonium salt?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a salt containing the ammonium ion.
explanation.principle: Ammonium salts contain NH₄⁺ as the cation.
steps: NH₄Cl is ammonium chloride.
Its positive ion is NH₄⁺.
The other salts contain different cations.
Therefore NH₄Cl is an ammonium salt.
explanation.simple_explanation: Ammonium salts are identified by NH₄⁺. Check our "Tutorial section" for common ions.

subtopic: Nitrogen: oxidation of ammonia to nitrogen(IV) oxide and trioxonitrate(V) acid
question: The industrial process for making nitric acid from ammonia is called the

explanation.question_restate: We are to name the process that converts ammonia into nitric acid.
explanation.principle: Different named industrial processes produce different important chemicals.
steps: Haber process makes ammonia.
Ostwald process uses ammonia to make nitric acid.
Contact process makes sulphuric acid.
Therefore the correct name is Ostwald process.
explanation.simple_explanation: Haber makes NH₃, Ostwald makes HNO₃. Check our "Tutorial section" for named industrial processes.

question: The process that returns nitrogen from nitrates in the soil to the atmosphere is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the step that returns nitrogen to the atmosphere.
explanation.principle: The nitrogen cycle includes a process that removes usable nitrates from soil.
steps: Denitrifying bacteria convert nitrates into gaseous nitrogen compounds.
This sends nitrogen back to the atmosphere.
It reduces the available nitrate in soil.
Therefore the process is denitrification.
explanation.simple_explanation: Denitrification returns nitrogen to the air. Check our "Tutorial section" for the nitrogen cycle.

question: Diamond and graphite are allotropes of carbon because they

options.B: contain only carbon but differ in structure

answer_text: contain only carbon but differ in structure

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why diamond and graphite are called allotropes.
explanation.principle: Allotropes are different structural forms of the same element.
steps: Both diamond and graphite contain only carbon atoms.
However, their atomic arrangements are different.
That difference leads to different properties.
So they are allotropes of carbon.
explanation.final_answer: B. contain only carbon but differ in structure
explanation.simple_explanation: Allotropy means same element, different structure. Check our "Tutorial section" for structure-property links.

question: Diamond is extremely hard mainly because it has

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why diamond is so hard.
explanation.principle: A giant covalent network has strong bonds throughout the structure.
steps: Each carbon atom in diamond is strongly covalently bonded to others.
The structure extends in three dimensions.
A great deal of energy is needed to break these bonds.
Therefore diamond is extremely hard.
explanation.final_answer: B. a giant three-dimensional covalent structure
explanation.simple_explanation: Diamond is hard because of its giant covalent network. Check our "Tutorial section" for structures.

question: Graphite conducts electricity because it contains

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why graphite conducts electricity.
explanation.principle: Conduction in graphite depends on delocalized electrons.
steps: In graphite, each carbon is bonded in layers.
One electron per carbon is delocalized and can move.
These electrons carry charge along the layers.
Therefore graphite conducts electricity.
explanation.simple_explanation: Graphite conducts because of delocalized electrons. Check our "Tutorial section" for allotrope properties.

question: Complete combustion of carbon gives

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the product of complete burning of carbon.
explanation.principle: Complete combustion means carbon burns in sufficient oxygen.
steps: With enough oxygen, carbon is fully oxidized.
The product is carbon(IV) oxide, CO₂.
Incomplete combustion would give CO.
Therefore CO₂ is correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: Complete combustion of carbon gives CO₂. Check our "Tutorial section" for combustion products.

question: Carbon(IV) oxide turns limewater milky because it forms

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the milkiness produced when CO₂ is passed through limewater.
explanation.principle: CO₂ reacts with calcium hydroxide solution to form insoluble calcium carbonate.
steps: Limewater is Ca(OH)₂ solution.
CO₂ reacts with it to produce CaCO₃ and water.
CaCO₃ is a white insoluble solid.
That precipitate makes the liquid milky.
explanation.simple_explanation: The white CaCO₃ precipitate causes the milkiness in limewater. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas tests.

question: Carbon(II) oxide is formed when carbon burns in

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the condition that forms carbon(II) oxide.
explanation.principle: Limited oxygen causes incomplete combustion.
steps: If enough oxygen is present, carbon forms CO₂.
If oxygen is limited, the oxidation is incomplete.
Then CO can be formed.
Therefore limited oxygen is correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: CO is produced during incomplete combustion. Check our "Tutorial section" for complete versus incomplete burning.

question: Carbon(II) oxide is poisonous because it

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why CO is poisonous.
explanation.principle: CO interferes with oxygen transport in blood.
steps: Haemoglobin normally carries oxygen.
CO binds much more strongly to haemoglobin than oxygen does.
This reduces oxygen delivery to body tissues.
Therefore CO poisoning is dangerous.
explanation.simple_explanation: CO is dangerous because it blocks oxygen transport. Check our "Tutorial section" for safety facts.

question: Coal is best described as

options.B: a naturally occurring carbonaceous material with impurities

answer_text: a naturally occurring carbonaceous material with impurities

explanation.question_restate: We are to describe coal correctly.
explanation.principle: Coal is not pure carbon even though carbon is its main component.
steps: Coal forms naturally from ancient plant matter.
It contains carbon together with hydrogen, sulphur, moisture and mineral impurities.
Its exact composition varies.
Therefore it is a carbonaceous material with impurities.
explanation.final_answer: B. a naturally occurring carbonaceous material with impurities
explanation.simple_explanation: Coal is carbon-rich but not pure. Check our "Tutorial section" for fossil fuel chemistry.

question: Coke is produced from coal by

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the process that produces coke.
explanation.principle: Coke is the solid residue left when coal is heated without air.
steps: Coal is strongly heated in the absence of air.
Volatile products are driven off.
A carbon-rich solid remains.
That solid is coke.
explanation.simple_explanation: Coke comes from destructive distillation of coal. Check our "Tutorial section" for coal products.

subtopic: Carbon: destructive distillation of wood and coal
question: The main reason air is excluded during destructive distillation of coal is to

options.B: prevent combustion and allow useful products to be collected

answer_text: prevent combustion and allow useful products to be collected

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why air is excluded during destructive distillation.
explanation.principle: The aim is thermal decomposition, not simple combustion.
steps: If air were present, the material would burn.
That would destroy many useful products.
Heating without air allows coal or wood to decompose into solid, liquid and gaseous products.
Therefore air is excluded to prevent combustion and permit collection of products.
explanation.final_answer: B. prevent combustion and allow useful products to be collected
explanation.simple_explanation: Destructive distillation needs heating without air. Check our "Tutorial section" for process principles.

question: Synthetic gas is mainly a mixture of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the main composition of synthetic gas.
explanation.principle: Syngas is an important industrial feedstock made mainly of two gases.
steps: The major components are carbon(II) oxide and hydrogen.
This mixture can be used to produce methanol and other chemicals.
That is why it is called synthesis gas.
Therefore CO and H₂ are correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: Syngas mainly contains CO and H₂. Check our "Tutorial section" for industrial gases.

question: A major industrial use of synthetic gas is in the production of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify an important use of syngas.
explanation.principle: Syngas is a feedstock for several industrial syntheses.
steps: CO and H₂ can be reacted to produce methanol.
This is one of the common industrial uses of syngas.
That is why the mixture is valuable in chemical manufacture.
Therefore methanol is correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: Syngas is widely used to make methanol. Check our "Tutorial section" for industrial applications.

question: If excess carbon(IV) oxide is passed through limewater, the milkiness disappears because

options.A: the CaCO₃ reacts further to form soluble calcium hydrogencarbonate

answer_text: the CaCO₃ reacts further to form soluble calcium hydrogencarbonate

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why milkiness disappears in excess CO₂.
explanation.principle: A precipitate can dissolve if a soluble product forms on further reaction.
steps: First, CO₂ forms insoluble CaCO₃ and the liquid becomes milky.
When more CO₂ is passed in, the CaCO₃ reacts further with water and CO₂.
A soluble hydrogencarbonate forms.
So the milkiness disappears.
explanation.final_answer: A. the CaCO₃ reacts further to form soluble calcium hydrogencarbonate
explanation.simple_explanation: Excess CO₂ can dissolve the first precipitate. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas-test exceptions.

question: Which statement about carbon(II) oxide is correct?

options.B: it is a neutral oxide and reducing agent

answer_text: it is a neutral oxide and reducing agent

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose a correct statement about CO.
explanation.principle: CO has a distinctive combination of classification and chemical behavior.
steps: CO is usually treated as a neutral oxide.
It can reduce some metal oxides to metals.
It does not itself give the usual limewater test of CO₂.
Therefore option B is correct.
explanation.final_answer: B. it is a neutral oxide and reducing agent
explanation.simple_explanation: CO is neutral as an oxide but useful as a reducing agent. Check our "Tutorial section" for oxide classes.

question: Graphite is soft and slippery because

options.A: its layers are held together by weak forces and can slide
options.C: it is made of separate atoms with no bonds

answer_text: its layers are held together by weak forces and can slide

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why graphite feels soft.
explanation.principle: Its layered structure allows easy sliding between layers.
steps: Within each layer, graphite has strong covalent bonds.
Between layers, the attractive forces are much weaker.
So the layers can slide over one another.
That gives graphite its softness and slippery feel.
explanation.final_answer: A. its layers are held together by weak forces and can slide
explanation.simple_explanation: Graphite is soft because its layers slide easily. Check our "Tutorial section" for structure-property relationships.

question: Coal gas, coal tar and coke are products of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the process that gives these well-known coal products.
explanation.principle: Heating coal without air produces several useful materials.
steps: Coke is the solid residue.
Coal tar is a liquid fraction.
Coal gas is a gaseous product.
All three come from destructive distillation of coal.
explanation.simple_explanation: Coke, coal tar and coal gas are standard coal-distillation products. Check our "Tutorial section" for product tables.

question: A major use of coke in metallurgy is as

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify an important use of coke.
explanation.principle: Coke helps provide heat and reducing conditions in furnaces.
steps: Coke is rich in carbon.
It can burn to supply heat.
It also helps generate reducing gases such as CO.
Therefore it is used as a reducing agent and fuel in metallurgy.
explanation.final_answer: A. a reducing agent and fuel
explanation.simple_explanation: Coke is important in metal extraction because it supplies heat and reducing action. Check our "Tutorial section" for metallurgy.

subtopic: Carbon: destructive distillation of wood and coal
question: Which product is associated more with destructive distillation of wood than with coal?

explanation.question_restate: We are to distinguish between products of wood and coal distillation.
explanation.principle: The names of the products often reflect the source material.
steps: Heating wood without air gives wood charcoal and related products.
Heating coal without air gives coke, coal gas and coal tar.
So wood charcoal is more associated with wood.
Therefore option A is correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: Keep wood products distinct from coal products in revision. Check our "Tutorial section" for comparison tables.

question: Synthetic gas is called a feedstock because it

options.B: serves as a starting material for making other chemicals

answer_text: serves as a starting material for making other chemicals

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why syngas is called a feedstock.
explanation.principle: A feedstock is a starting raw material used to produce other substances.
steps: Syngas contains CO and H₂.
These gases can be processed to make methanol and other compounds.
So the mixture is not just a fuel; it is also a chemical starting material.
Therefore it serves as a feedstock for making other chemicals.
explanation.final_answer: B. serves as a starting material for making other chemicals
explanation.simple_explanation: Syngas is important because it is a flexible chemical feedstock. Check our "Tutorial section" for industrial chemistry.

question: Which statement best compares carbon(IV) oxide and carbon(II) oxide?

options.A: both are acidic oxides and both turn limewater milky directly
options.B: CO₂ is an acidic oxide, while CO is a neutral oxide and reducing agent
options.C: CO₂ is poisonous because it combines strongly with haemoglobin, while CO is not

answer_text: CO₂ is an acidic oxide, while CO is a neutral oxide and reducing agent

explanation.question_restate: We are to compare the two common oxides of carbon.
explanation.principle: Carbon(IV) oxide and carbon(II) oxide differ clearly in both classification and behavior.
steps: CO₂ is usually classified as an acidic oxide because it forms an acid in water and reacts with bases.
CO is usually classified as a neutral oxide.
CO can also act as a reducing agent in metallurgical processes.
Therefore the best comparison is that CO₂ is acidic, while CO is neutral and reducing.
explanation.final_answer: B. CO₂ is an acidic oxide, while CO is a neutral oxide and reducing agent
explanation.simple_explanation: This comparison is very common in exam questions on carbon compounds. Check our "Tutorial section" for side-by-side revision tables.

subtopic: Hydrogen: commercial production from water gas and petroleum cracking
question: Water gas is important in hydrogen manufacture because it contains

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the composition of water gas.
explanation.principle: Water gas is the mixture formed when steam reacts with red-hot carbon.
steps: Steam reacts with hot carbon or coke.
The main products are carbon(II) oxide and hydrogen.
Because hydrogen is already present, the mixture is useful industrially.
Therefore water gas contains CO and H₂.
explanation.simple_explanation: Water gas is mainly CO and H₂. Check our "Tutorial section" for industrial gases.

subtopic: Hydrogen: commercial production from water gas and petroleum cracking
question: The main purpose of the water-gas shift reaction is to

options.D: decrease the amount of steam used

explanation.question_restate: We are to state the purpose of the water-gas shift reaction.
explanation.principle: The reaction converts CO and steam into CO₂ and extra H₂.
steps: Water gas contains both CO and H₂.
CO can react further with steam.
This produces more hydrogen while CO₂ can later be removed.
So the aim is to increase hydrogen yield.
explanation.simple_explanation: The shift reaction is used to make extra hydrogen. Check our "Tutorial section" for industrial preparation.

subtopic: Hydrogen: commercial production from water gas and petroleum cracking
question: Hydrogen can also be obtained commercially from hydrocarbons by

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify another commercial source of hydrogen.
explanation.principle: Hydrocarbons can be processed to give hydrogen-rich mixtures.
steps: Petroleum fractions contain hydrocarbons.
Cracking and reforming produce smaller molecules and gas mixtures.
Further treatment such as steam reforming can yield hydrogen.
Therefore cracking and reforming are linked to hydrogen production.
explanation.simple_explanation: Hydrogen can be obtained from hydrocarbon feedstocks after processing. Check our "Tutorial section" for industrial links.

question: A common laboratory method for preparing hydrogen is by reacting zinc with dilute

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a common acid used in laboratory preparation of hydrogen.
explanation.principle: A reactive metal above hydrogen can liberate hydrogen from dilute hydrochloric acid.
steps: Zinc is more reactive than hydrogen.
It reacts with dilute hydrochloric acid to form zinc chloride and hydrogen.
This is a standard school-laboratory method.
Therefore dilute HCl is used.
explanation.simple_explanation: Zinc and dilute HCl are commonly used to prepare H₂ in the lab. Check our "Tutorial section" for setups and precautions.

question: Why is copper unsuitable for the ordinary laboratory preparation of hydrogen from dilute HCl?

options.B: copper is below hydrogen in the reactivity series

answer_text: copper is below hydrogen in the reactivity series

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why copper is not used to produce hydrogen from dilute hydrochloric acid.
explanation.principle: Only metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series usually displace hydrogen from dilute acids.
steps: Copper is less reactive than hydrogen in this context.
So it does not readily displace hydrogen from dilute HCl.
Metals like zinc and magnesium are used instead.
Therefore copper is unsuitable because it lies below hydrogen in the reactivity series.
explanation.final_answer: B. copper is below hydrogen in the reactivity series
explanation.simple_explanation: Reactivity series ideas help explain why some metals produce H₂ and others do not. Check our "Tutorial section" for the series.

question: The gas evolved when magnesium reacts with dilute HCl is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the gas released when magnesium reacts with dilute hydrochloric acid.
explanation.principle: Reactive metals displace hydrogen from dilute acids.
steps: Magnesium is above hydrogen in the reactivity series.
It reacts with HCl to form magnesium chloride.
Hydrogen gas is liberated.
Therefore the gas evolved is H₂.
explanation.simple_explanation: Metal plus dilute acid often gives salt plus hydrogen. Check our "Tutorial section" for reaction patterns.

question: Hydrogen is commonly described as

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose a correct physical description of hydrogen.
explanation.principle: Hydrogen is a simple diatomic gas with no colour or smell.
steps: Hydrogen has no colour.
It has no odour.
It is also very light compared with air.
Therefore it is a colourless, odourless gas.
explanation.simple_explanation: Hydrogen is remembered as a colourless, odourless gas. Check our "Tutorial section" for quick property tables.

question: Hydrogen is a reducing agent because it can

options.A: remove oxygen from some metallic oxides

answer_text: remove oxygen from some metallic oxides

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the reducing property of hydrogen.
explanation.principle: A reducing agent can remove oxygen from another substance.
steps: Hot hydrogen can react with some metal oxides.
For example, it reduces copper(II) oxide to copper.
Water is formed as hydrogen takes the oxygen.
So hydrogen acts as a reducing agent.
explanation.final_answer: A. remove oxygen from some metallic oxides
explanation.simple_explanation: Hydrogen reduces some oxides by taking away oxygen. Check our "Tutorial section" for redox examples.

question: A major industrial use of hydrogen is in the manufacture of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify an important industrial use of hydrogen.
explanation.principle: Hydrogen combines with nitrogen in the Haber process to form ammonia.
steps: The Haber process uses N₂ and H₂.
The product is ammonia, NH₃.
Ammonia is needed for fertilizer and chemical manufacture.
Therefore hydrogen is used in making NH₃.
explanation.simple_explanation: One key industrial use of hydrogen is ammonia manufacture. Check our "Tutorial section" for the Haber process.

question: Hydrogen is used to harden vegetable oils because it

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the role of hydrogen in hardening vegetable oils.
explanation.principle: Unsaturated oils contain carbon-carbon double bonds that can undergo addition.
steps: The C=C bonds in unsaturated oils can react with hydrogen.
Hydrogen adds across those double bonds in the presence of a catalyst.
This makes the oil more saturated and harder.
Therefore hydrogen adds across C=C bonds.
explanation.simple_explanation: Hydrogenation is an addition reaction across double bonds. Check our "Tutorial section" for organic applications.

question: Which property of hydrogen makes it useful in weather balloons?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the property responsible for lift in balloons.
explanation.principle: A gas rises if it is less dense than the surrounding air.
steps: Hydrogen is very light.
Its density is lower than that of air.
This gives a balloon filled with hydrogen strong upthrust.
Therefore low density is the useful property.
explanation.simple_explanation: Hydrogen gives lift because it is very light. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas uses.

question: The standard test for hydrogen is that it

explanation.question_restate: We are to recall the usual laboratory test for hydrogen.
explanation.principle: Hydrogen burns rapidly in air and gives a characteristic sound.
steps: A lighted splint is brought near the gas.
The gas burns quickly with oxygen in air.
A small pop sound is heard.
That squeaky pop confirms hydrogen.
explanation.final_answer: B. burns with a squeaky pop
explanation.simple_explanation: The pop test is the classic test for hydrogen. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas tests.

question: The pop heard during the hydrogen test is due to

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the origin of the pop sound in the hydrogen test.
explanation.principle: The sound is produced by rapid burning of hydrogen in air.
steps: Hydrogen is combustible.
When a flame is applied, it reacts quickly with oxygen.
This rapid combustion gives a small explosive pop.
So the sound is due to fast reaction with oxygen.
explanation.simple_explanation: The pop comes from quick burning with oxygen in the air. Check our "Tutorial section" for test explanations.

question: The first gas collected in a hydrogen preparation is usually not tested immediately because it may contain

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why the first gas from a hydrogen apparatus is usually discarded.
explanation.principle: At the beginning, the flask and tube contain air.
steps: When the reaction starts, hydrogen mixes with the trapped air.
This mixture can be explosive if ignited.
Therefore the first portion is allowed to escape.
So the reason is that it may contain air.
explanation.simple_explanation: The first gas is often a mixture with air, so it is discarded. Check our "Tutorial section" for safety.

question: Hydrogen burns in oxygen to form

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the product of burning hydrogen in oxygen.
explanation.principle: Hydrogen combustion gives water.
steps: Combustion means reaction with oxygen.
The equation is 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O.
The product may be seen as steam or condensed droplets.
Therefore hydrogen forms water when it burns.
explanation.simple_explanation: Hydrogen combustion produces water. Check our "Tutorial section" for combustion equations.

subtopic: Hydrogen: commercial production from water gas and petroleum cracking
question: Which gas is commonly removed from the mixture after the water-gas shift reaction to leave a purer hydrogen stream?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the gas usually removed after the shift reaction.
explanation.principle: The shift reaction produces CO₂ and H₂ from CO and steam.
steps: Hydrogen is the desired product.
Carbon(IV) oxide is an unwanted by-product in the hydrogen stream.
Removing CO₂ increases purity of the hydrogen obtained.
Therefore CO₂ is the gas removed.
explanation.simple_explanation: Hydrogen is purified after the shift reaction by removing CO₂. Check our "Tutorial section" for industrial separation.

question: Helium is often preferred to hydrogen in balloons mainly because helium is

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why helium is often chosen instead of hydrogen for balloons.
explanation.principle: Safety is a major practical consideration.
steps: Hydrogen gives good lift but is highly flammable.
Helium is also light but is inert and non-flammable.
This makes helium safer in many applications.
Therefore helium is preferred because it is non-flammable.
explanation.simple_explanation: Safety often matters more than maximum lift. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas comparisons.

question: Which balanced equation correctly represents the reaction between zinc and dilute HCl?

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose the correct balanced equation for preparing hydrogen from zinc and hydrochloric acid.
explanation.principle: Zinc forms Zn²⁺ ions and hydrogen ions combine to make H₂.
steps: Zinc reacts to form zinc chloride, ZnCl₂.
Two HCl molecules are needed to supply the two chloride ions and the hydrogen ions.
The hydrogen ions combine to form H₂.
Therefore Zn + 2HCl → ZnCl₂ + H₂ is correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: Balancing the equation helps show both the salt and gas clearly. Check our "Tutorial section" for worked equations.

question: Compared with air, hydrogen diffuses rapidly mainly because its molecules are

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why hydrogen diffuses quickly.
explanation.principle: Lighter gas particles move faster and spread more rapidly.
steps: Hydrogen molecules have very small mass.
This gives them high average speed at the same temperature.
So the gas spreads quickly by diffusion.
Therefore the key reason is that the molecules are very light.
explanation.simple_explanation: Hydrogen diffuses rapidly because its molecules are very light. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas behavior.

question: Why should the test tube be pointed away from the face when testing hydrogen with a flame?

options.B: the gas may ignite suddenly with a pop
options.D: the gas will turn the flame green

answer_text: the gas may ignite suddenly with a pop

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain an important safety rule in the hydrogen test.
explanation.principle: Hydrogen may burn suddenly when a flame is applied.
steps: The gas mixes with oxygen near the mouth of the tube.
If it is hydrogen, a pop may occur on ignition.
For safety, the tube must not face anyone.
Therefore the tube is pointed away because the gas may ignite suddenly.
explanation.final_answer: B. the gas may ignite suddenly with a pop
explanation.simple_explanation: Safety is important because hydrogen can ignite quickly. Check our "Tutorial section" for laboratory precautions.

question: Chlorine belongs to the halogens because it

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify why chlorine is classed as a halogen.
explanation.principle: Halogens are Group 17 elements with seven outer-shell electrons.
steps: Chlorine has seven electrons in its valence shell.
This makes it similar to fluorine, bromine and iodine.
Such elements tend to gain one electron in simple ionic compounds.
Therefore chlorine is a halogen because it has seven valence electrons.
explanation.simple_explanation: Group 17 elements have seven valence electrons. Check our "Tutorial section" for periodicity.

question: A typical chemical behavior of chlorine as a halogen is that it

options.A: forms Cl⁻ by gaining one electron
options.B: forms Na⁺ by losing one electron

answer_text: forms Cl⁻ by gaining one electron

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose a behavior that shows chlorine acting like a halogen.
explanation.principle: Halogens usually gain one electron to complete their octet.
steps: Chlorine needs one extra electron to complete its outer shell.
It commonly forms chloride ion, Cl⁻.
This is typical of Group 17 chemistry.
Therefore chlorine forms Cl⁻ by gaining one electron.
explanation.final_answer: A. forms Cl⁻ by gaining one electron
explanation.simple_explanation: Chlorine behaves like a typical halogen by forming chloride ions. Check our "Tutorial section" for group trends.

question: Chlorine is prepared in the laboratory by heating concentrated HCl with

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the solid reagent used with concentrated HCl in preparing chlorine.
explanation.principle: MnO₂ acts as the oxidizing agent in this preparation.
steps: Concentrated HCl supplies chloride ions.
Manganese(IV) oxide oxidizes the chloride to chlorine.
This is a standard laboratory method.
Therefore the solid reagent is MnO₂.
explanation.simple_explanation: MnO₂ and concentrated HCl are commonly used to prepare chlorine. Check our "Tutorial section" for laboratory methods.

question: Chlorine is usually collected by downward delivery because it is

options.B: denser than air and fairly soluble in water

answer_text: denser than air and fairly soluble in water

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the method of collecting chlorine gas.
explanation.principle: Gas collection depends strongly on density and solubility.
steps: Chlorine is denser than air.
It is also fairly soluble in water and reacts with it.
So collection over water is unsuitable.
Therefore chlorine is collected by downward delivery.
explanation.final_answer: B. denser than air and fairly soluble in water
explanation.simple_explanation: Chlorine is collected by downward delivery because water is unsuitable. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas collection.

question: Industrial chlorine is obtained mainly by electrolysis of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the industrial source of chlorine.
explanation.principle: Brine is concentrated aqueous sodium chloride.
steps: Brine is passed through an electrolytic cell.
Chlorine is liberated at the anode.
Hydrogen and sodium hydroxide are also formed.
Therefore industrial chlorine is obtained from brine.
explanation.simple_explanation: Electrolysis of brine is the main industrial source of chlorine. Check our "Tutorial section" for electrolysis.

question: During the electrolysis of concentrated sodium chloride solution, chlorine is evolved at the

explanation.question_restate: We are to state the electrode where chlorine is formed in brine electrolysis.
explanation.principle: Oxidation takes place at the anode.
steps: Chloride ions move to the positive electrode.
There they lose electrons to form Cl₂.
This is oxidation.
Therefore chlorine is evolved at the anode.
explanation.simple_explanation: Chloride ions are oxidized at the anode to chlorine. Check our "Tutorial section" for electrode processes.

question: One common use of chlorine is in

explanation.question_restate: We are to recall a common use of chlorine.
explanation.principle: Chlorine kills many harmful microorganisms in water.
steps: Small quantities of chlorine or chlorine compounds are added to water.
These destroy many disease-causing organisms.
This is a major use in public water treatment.
Therefore water sterilization is correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: Chlorine is widely used to disinfect water. Check our "Tutorial section" for practical applications.

question: The bleaching action of chlorine is due mainly to

options.A: its oxidizing action in moist conditions
options.B: its reducing action in dry conditions

answer_text: its oxidizing action in moist conditions

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why chlorine bleaches many coloured materials.
explanation.principle: Moisture is needed for effective bleaching by chlorine.
steps: Chlorine reacts with water to form active oxidizing species.
These oxidize colouring matter to colourless substances.
Dry chlorine shows little bleaching effect.
Therefore bleaching is due mainly to oxidation in moist conditions.
explanation.final_answer: A. its oxidizing action in moist conditions
explanation.simple_explanation: Chlorine bleaches by oxidation when moisture is present. Check our "Tutorial section" for bleaching agents.

subtopic: Halogens: hydrogen chloride and hydrochloric acid
question: Hydrogen chloride gas becomes hydrochloric acid when it

explanation.question_restate: We are to distinguish between hydrogen chloride gas and hydrochloric acid.
explanation.principle: Hydrochloric acid is the aqueous solution of hydrogen chloride.
steps: Hydrogen chloride in the gaseous state is HCl gas.
When dissolved in water, it ionizes to form an acidic solution.
That aqueous solution is called hydrochloric acid.
Therefore dissolution in water is the key step.
explanation.simple_explanation: HCl gas becomes hydrochloric acid in water. Check our "Tutorial section" for ionization and acids.

subtopic: Halogens: hydrogen chloride and hydrochloric acid
question: Dry HCl gas does not turn dry blue litmus red because

options.B: it does not ionize without water

answer_text: it does not ionize without water

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why dry hydrogen chloride does not show the usual acidic effect on dry litmus.
explanation.principle: The indicator reaction depends on the presence of ions in water.
steps: Dry HCl is covalent.
Without water, the ions responsible for acidity are not produced in the normal way.
So dry blue litmus does not turn red.
Therefore lack of ionization without water is the reason.
explanation.final_answer: B. it does not ionize without water
explanation.simple_explanation: Water is needed for the usual litmus acid test. Check our "Tutorial section" for indicator behavior.

subtopic: Halogens: chlorides and test for chlorides
question: A solution suspected to contain chloride ions is tested with acidified

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the reagent for chloride ions.
explanation.principle: Silver nitrate forms a characteristic precipitate with chloride ions.
steps: The solution is first acidified, usually with dilute nitric acid.
Silver nitrate is then added.
A white precipitate of AgCl indicates chloride ions.
Therefore acidified AgNO₃ is used.
explanation.simple_explanation: Acidified silver nitrate is the standard test for chloride ions. Check our "Tutorial section" for anion tests.

subtopic: Halogens: chlorides and test for chlorides
question: The white precipitate formed when AgNO₃ is added to a chloride solution is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the white precipitate in the chloride test.
explanation.principle: Silver ions combine with chloride ions to form silver chloride.
steps: Ag⁺ comes from AgNO₃.
Cl⁻ comes from the sample.
The insoluble product is AgCl.
Therefore the white precipitate is silver chloride.
explanation.simple_explanation: Silver chloride is the white precipitate in the chloride test. Check our "Tutorial section" for precipitate colors.

question: Chlorine gas is best described as

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the physical description of chlorine gas.
explanation.principle: JAMB-style questions often test gases by appearance and smell.
steps: Chlorine is greenish-yellow in colour.
It has a choking smell.
It is also poisonous.
Therefore option B is correct.
explanation.final_answer: B. greenish-yellow with a choking smell
explanation.simple_explanation: Colour and smell are useful quick identifiers for gases. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas summaries.

question: Why is moist chlorine more effective as a bleaching agent than dry chlorine?

options.A: moisture helps produce the active oxidizing species
options.D: dry chlorine becomes heavier than air

answer_text: moisture helps produce the active oxidizing species

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why bleaching by chlorine is stronger in moist conditions.
explanation.principle: Water helps chlorine form oxidizing agents responsible for bleaching.
steps: Dry chlorine has little bleaching action.
In the presence of water, chlorine gives species such as hypochlorous acid.
These oxidize coloured materials.
Therefore moisture helps produce the active oxidizing species.
explanation.final_answer: A. moisture helps produce the active oxidizing species
explanation.simple_explanation: Moisture activates the bleaching action of chlorine. Check our "Tutorial section" for the chemistry behind bleaching.

subtopic: Halogens: hydrogen chloride and hydrochloric acid
question: Which gas gives dense white fumes with ammonia gas?
