explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the gas that produces dense white fumes with ammonia.
explanation.principle: Hydrogen chloride reacts with ammonia to form solid ammonium chloride.
steps: Ammonia gas is NH₃.
Hydrogen chloride gas is HCl.
They combine to form NH₄Cl, which appears as dense white fumes.
Therefore the gas is HCl.
explanation.simple_explanation: Dense white fumes with ammonia are a classic test for HCl gas. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas identification.

question: A major by-product formed together with chlorine during electrolysis of brine is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify another product of brine electrolysis.
explanation.principle: The cathode reaction in aqueous brine produces hydrogen gas.
steps: At the anode, chloride gives chlorine.
At the cathode, water is reduced and hydrogen forms.
Sodium hydroxide also remains in solution.
Therefore hydrogen is formed together with chlorine.
explanation.simple_explanation: Electrolysis of brine gives chlorine and hydrogen among the products. Check our "Tutorial section" for cell products.

subtopic: Halogens: chlorides and test for chlorides
question: Why is dilute nitric acid used before adding AgNO₃ in the chloride test rather than dilute HCl?

options.A: HCl would supply chloride ions and cause a false result
options.C: HCl dissolves all silver salts permanently
options.D: HNO₃ is the only acid that exists

answer_text: HCl would supply chloride ions and cause a false result

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the choice of acid in the chloride test.
explanation.principle: The reagent used must not supply the ion being tested for.
steps: Hydrochloric acid contains chloride ions.
Using it would produce AgCl even if the sample had no chloride.
That would give a false positive.
Therefore nitric acid is used instead.
explanation.final_answer: A. HCl would supply chloride ions and cause a false result
explanation.simple_explanation: Never use a reagent that introduces the ion you are testing for. Check our "Tutorial section" for practical analysis logic.

question: Chlorine water fades in sunlight mainly because chlorine

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why chlorine water loses colour on standing in sunlight.
explanation.principle: Chlorine water is unstable in light.
steps: Dissolved chlorine and its products in water undergo further reactions.
Sunlight speeds up these changes.
The concentration of dissolved chlorine falls.
Therefore the colour fades because chlorine is destroyed in light-driven reactions.
explanation.final_answer: A. is destroyed in light-driven reactions
explanation.simple_explanation: Chlorine water fades because it is unstable in light. Check our "Tutorial section" for common observations.

question: Which statement best shows chlorine acting as a halogen?

options.A: it forms chlorides with many elements
options.B: it always forms only covalent oxides
options.D: it has no common negative ion

answer_text: it forms chlorides with many elements

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a behavior that is typical of a halogen.
explanation.principle: Halogens characteristically combine with other elements to form halides.
steps: Chlorine forms compounds such as NaCl, MgCl₂ and HCl.
These are chlorides or chloride-containing compounds.
This reflects typical halogen chemistry.
Therefore forming chlorides with many elements best shows chlorine acting as a halogen.
explanation.final_answer: A. it forms chlorides with many elements
explanation.simple_explanation: Halogens are recognized by their tendency to form halides. Check our "Tutorial section" for group chemistry.

subtopic: Halogens: chlorides and test for chlorides
question: Which salt will give a positive test with acidified silver nitrate for chloride ions?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a salt that contains chloride ions.
explanation.principle: Any soluble chloride can give the silver nitrate test for chloride ions.
steps: Ammonium chloride contains Cl⁻ ions.
Acidified AgNO₃ reacts with chloride to form white AgCl.
The other salts listed do not contain chloride ions.
Therefore NH₄Cl will give a positive chloride test.
explanation.simple_explanation: A positive chloride test requires Cl⁻ ions in the solution. Check our "Tutorial section" for anion tests.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: oxygen: preparation, properties, uses, commercial production from liquid air
question: In the laboratory, oxygen is commonly prepared by heating KClO₃ with

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the substance used with KClO₃ in preparing oxygen.
explanation.principle: Manganese(IV) oxide is the catalyst used in this decomposition.
steps: Potassium trioxochlorate(V) decomposes on heating.
The products include potassium chloride and oxygen.
Manganese(IV) oxide speeds the decomposition.
Therefore MnO₂ is used.
explanation.simple_explanation: KClO₃ with MnO₂ is a standard laboratory source of oxygen. Check our "Tutorial section" for preparation methods.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: oxygen: preparation, properties, uses, commercial production from liquid air
question: Oxygen is collected over water because it is

options.A: highly soluble and reactive with water
options.B: only slightly soluble and does not react much with water during collection

answer_text: only slightly soluble and does not react much with water during collection

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the use of water displacement for collecting oxygen.
explanation.principle: A gas collected over water should be only slightly soluble and not react appreciably with water.
steps: Oxygen has low solubility in water.
It does not react significantly with water in the collection setup.
So it can displace water and be collected.
Therefore option B is correct.
explanation.final_answer: B. only slightly soluble and does not react much with water during collection
explanation.simple_explanation: Oxygen can be collected over water because little dissolves. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas collection.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: oxygen: preparation, properties, uses, commercial production from liquid air
question: Commercial oxygen is obtained from liquid air by

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the industrial method for obtaining oxygen from air.
explanation.principle: Liquid air can be separated because its components have different boiling points.
steps: Air is first liquefied.
The liquid mixture is then fractionally distilled.
Oxygen and nitrogen separate based on boiling-point differences.
Therefore oxygen is obtained by fractional distillation.
explanation.simple_explanation: Industrial oxygen comes from liquid air by fractional distillation. Check our "Tutorial section" for separation processes.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: oxygen: preparation, properties, uses, commercial production from liquid air
question: A glowing splint relights in oxygen because oxygen

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the glowing-splint test for oxygen.
explanation.principle: Oxygen allows materials to burn more vigorously.
steps: A glowing splint is still hot and partly burning.
When placed in oxygen, combustion becomes stronger.
The splint bursts into flame again.
Therefore oxygen supports combustion strongly.
explanation.simple_explanation: Oxygen relights a glowing splint because it supports combustion. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas tests.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: oxides: acidic, basic, amphoteric and neutral
question: Which oxide is acidic?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify an acidic oxide.
explanation.principle: Acidic oxides are often non-metal oxides that react with bases or form acids with water.
steps: CO₂ is an oxide of a non-metal.
It reacts with bases such as NaOH or limewater.
This is typical acidic-oxide behavior.
Therefore CO₂ is acidic.
explanation.simple_explanation: Many non-metal oxides are acidic. Check our "Tutorial section" for oxide classification.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: oxides: acidic, basic, amphoteric and neutral
question: Which pair is correctly matched?

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose the correct oxide classification.
explanation.principle: Amphoteric oxides react with both acids and bases.
steps: Aluminium oxide, Al₂O₃, is amphoteric.
CO is usually neutral, not acidic.
SO₃ is acidic, not neutral.
Na₂O is basic, not amphoteric.
explanation.simple_explanation: Al₂O₃ is a standard example of an amphoteric oxide. Check our "Tutorial section" for oxide types.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: ozone as an allotrope and its atmospheric importance
question: Ozone differs from ordinary oxygen because ozone molecules contain

explanation.question_restate: We are to distinguish ozone from ordinary oxygen.
explanation.principle: Ozone is O₃ while ordinary oxygen is O₂.
steps: Both are forms of the element oxygen.
The difference lies in the number of oxygen atoms per molecule.
Ozone has three atoms in each molecule.
Therefore the correct answer is three oxygen atoms.
explanation.simple_explanation: Ozone is O₃, while ordinary oxygen is O₂. Check our "Tutorial section" for allotropy.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: ozone as an allotrope and its atmospheric importance
question: The major benefit of the ozone layer is that it

explanation.question_restate: We are to state the main importance of atmospheric ozone.
explanation.principle: The ozone layer protects living things from much UV radiation.
steps: Ozone high in the atmosphere absorbs significant ultraviolet rays.
This reduces the amount reaching Earth’s surface.
That protection helps living organisms.
Therefore the ozone layer absorbs harmful ultraviolet radiation.
explanation.simple_explanation: The ozone layer protects Earth by screening UV radiation. Check our "Tutorial section" for atmospheric chemistry.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: sulphur: allotropes and uses
question: Rhombic sulphur and monoclinic sulphur are

explanation.question_restate: We are to classify rhombic sulphur and monoclinic sulphur.
explanation.principle: Allotropes are different structural forms of the same element.
steps: Both substances contain only sulphur atoms.
They differ in crystal structure and stability conditions.
So they are not different elements or isotopes.
Therefore they are allotropes.
explanation.simple_explanation: Rhombic and monoclinic sulphur are allotropes. Check our "Tutorial section" for sulphur forms.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: sulphur: allotropes and uses
question: At room temperature, the more stable crystalline form of sulphur is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the more stable allotrope of sulphur at room temperature.
explanation.principle: Different allotropes are stable under different conditions.
steps: Rhombic sulphur is stable at ordinary room temperature.
Monoclinic sulphur is stable at higher temperatures.
That is why monoclinic sulphur changes to rhombic on standing at room temperature.
Therefore rhombic sulphur is the stable form.
explanation.simple_explanation: Rhombic sulphur is the stable room-temperature allotrope. Check our "Tutorial section" for allotrope stability.

question: Sulphur(IV) oxide is produced when sulphur burns in

explanation.question_restate: We are to state how SO₂ is produced from sulphur.
explanation.principle: Burning sulphur in oxygen gives sulphur(IV) oxide.
steps: Combustion is reaction with oxygen.
Sulphur burns with a blue flame.
The gaseous product is SO₂.
Therefore oxygen is the correct answer.
explanation.simple_explanation: Burning sulphur produces sulphur(IV) oxide. Check our "Tutorial section" for sulphur compounds.

question: The bleaching action of sulphur(IV) oxide is mainly due to its

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why sulphur(IV) oxide can bleach some materials.
explanation.principle: SO₂ bleaches by reduction rather than oxidation.
steps: SO₂ can reduce coloured substances to colourless forms.
This is different from chlorine, which bleaches mainly by oxidation.
The effect is often reversible.
Therefore its reducing property is responsible.
explanation.simple_explanation: SO₂ bleaches by reduction. Check our "Tutorial section" for comparing bleaching agents.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: trioxosulphate(IV) acid and salts
question: The acid formed when SO₂ dissolves in water is represented by

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the acid formed from sulphur(IV) oxide in water.
explanation.principle: SO₂ is an acidic oxide and gives sulphurous acid in water.
steps: SO₂ dissolves in water.
The corresponding acid is written as H₂SO₃.
Its salts are sulphites.
Therefore the acid formed is H₂SO₃.
explanation.simple_explanation: SO₂ in water gives H₂SO₃ solution. Check our "Tutorial section" for oxide-to-acid relationships.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: tetraoxosulphate(VI) acid, including contact process
question: In the contact process, SO₂ is oxidized to SO₃ using a catalyst of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the catalyst used in the contact process.
explanation.principle: V₂O₅ is the standard catalyst for the oxidation of SO₂.
steps: SO₂ is converted to SO₃ in the process.
The reaction is speeded up by vanadium(V) oxide.
The catalyst helps the system reach equilibrium faster.
Therefore V₂O₅ is correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: V₂O₅ is the contact-process catalyst. Check our "Tutorial section" for named industrial processes.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: tetraoxosulphate(VI) acid, including contact process
question: SO₃ is first absorbed in concentrated H₂SO₄ during the contact process to form

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the product formed when SO₃ is absorbed in concentrated sulphuric acid.
explanation.principle: The contact process avoids direct absorption of SO₃ in water.
steps: SO₃ reacts with concentrated H₂SO₄.
The product is H₂S₂O₇, called oleum.
Oleum is later diluted to produce more H₂SO₄.
Therefore the product is oleum.
explanation.simple_explanation: Oleum is formed before the final dilution step in the contact process. Check our "Tutorial section" for process flow.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: test for sulphate
question: A reagent used to test for sulphate ions is acidified

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the reagent for sulphate ions.
explanation.principle: Barium ions form an insoluble white precipitate with sulphate ions.
steps: The test solution is acidified first.
Barium chloride is then added.
If sulphate is present, BaSO₄ forms as a white precipitate.
Therefore acidified BaCl₂ is used.
explanation.simple_explanation: Acidified barium chloride is used to test for sulphate ions. Check our "Tutorial section" for anion tests.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: hydrogen sulphide: preparation, properties and test for sulphide
question: Hydrogen sulphide is recognized by its characteristic

options.D: ability to relight a glowing splint

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a common property of hydrogen sulphide.
explanation.principle: H₂S has a well-known offensive smell.
steps: The gas is colourless.
Its smell is like rotten eggs.
This is one of its most familiar identifying features.
Therefore option B is correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: H₂S is known for its rotten-egg smell. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas properties.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: hydrogen sulphide: preparation, properties and test for sulphide
question: Hydrogen sulphide turns moist lead(II) ethanoate paper black because it forms

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the black substance formed in the H₂S test.
explanation.principle: Hydrogen sulphide reacts with lead(II) compounds to form lead(II) sulphide.
steps: Lead(II) ethanoate paper contains lead(II) ions.
H₂S supplies sulphide ions.
The black product formed is PbS.
Therefore PbS is responsible for the blackening.
explanation.simple_explanation: The blackening in the H₂S test is due to PbS. Check our "Tutorial section" for confirmatory tests.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: oxygen: preparation, properties, uses, commercial production from liquid air
question: Which statement about oxygen is correct?

options.B: it supports combustion but is not itself usually described as the fuel
options.C: it gives a squeaky pop with a flame
options.D: it forms dense white fumes with NH₃

answer_text: it supports combustion but is not itself usually described as the fuel

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose a correct statement about oxygen.
explanation.principle: Oxygen is the oxidizing medium in ordinary combustion.
steps: Many substances burn in oxygen.
The burning substance is the fuel, not oxygen itself.
This explains the glowing-splint test.
Therefore option B is correct.
explanation.final_answer: B. it supports combustion but is not itself usually described as the fuel
explanation.simple_explanation: Oxygen supports combustion rather than acting as the usual fuel. Check our "Tutorial section" for combustion basics.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: test for sulphate
question: The white precipitate formed when sulphate ions react with barium ions is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the solid formed in the sulphate test.
explanation.principle: Barium ions combine with sulphate ions to form an insoluble white precipitate.
steps: The test for sulphate uses acidified barium chloride.
If sulphate ions are present, Ba²⁺ and SO₄²⁻ combine.
The product is barium sulphate, BaSO₄.
This white precipitate confirms sulphate ions.
explanation.simple_explanation: BaSO₄ is the classic white precipitate used to confirm sulphate ions. Check our "Tutorial section" for anion tests.

question: Nitrogen can be prepared in the laboratory by warming a mixture that forms ammonium nitrite, which decomposes to

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the products of decomposition of ammonium nitrite.
explanation.principle: Ammonium nitrite is unstable and breaks down to nitrogen and water.
steps: In the laboratory it is often formed in situ.
On warming, it decomposes.
The gaseous product is nitrogen.
Therefore the products are N₂ and H₂O.
explanation.simple_explanation: Ammonium nitrite decomposes to nitrogen and water. Check our "Tutorial section" for lab preparation.

question: Commercial nitrogen is obtained mainly by

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the industrial source of nitrogen.
explanation.principle: Air is the main source of industrial nitrogen.
steps: Air is first liquefied.
The liquid air is then fractionally distilled.
Nitrogen separates because of its lower boiling point compared with oxygen.
Therefore fractional distillation of liquid air is correct.
explanation.final_answer: B. fractional distillation of liquid air
explanation.simple_explanation: Large-scale nitrogen comes from liquid air. Check our "Tutorial section" for industrial separation.

subtopic: Nitrogen: ammonia: Haber process, properties, uses
question: The Haber process combines nitrogen with

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the second reactant in the Haber process.
explanation.principle: Ammonia is made from nitrogen and hydrogen.
steps: The balanced equation is N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃.
Hydrogen supplies the hydrogen atoms in ammonia.
This is the basis of the Haber process.
Therefore nitrogen combines with hydrogen.
explanation.simple_explanation: The Haber process uses nitrogen and hydrogen. Check our "Tutorial section" for industrial processes.

subtopic: Nitrogen: ammonia: Haber process, properties, uses
question: Which catalyst is used in the Haber process?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the catalyst used in ammonia manufacture.
explanation.principle: Iron is the standard catalyst for the Haber process.
steps: The reaction would be too slow without a catalyst.
Finely divided iron speeds the process.
It helps equilibrium be reached faster.
Therefore iron is the catalyst.
explanation.simple_explanation: Iron is the classic Haber-process catalyst. Check our "Tutorial section" for named industrial catalysts.

subtopic: Nitrogen: ammonia: Haber process, properties, uses
question: Ammonia has a characteristic

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a common physical property of ammonia.
explanation.principle: Ammonia is a colourless gas with a sharp pungent smell.
steps: The smell is one of the easiest features to notice.
Ammonia is also alkaline in moist conditions.
It is not greenish-yellow or brown.
Therefore the correct answer is pungent smell.
explanation.simple_explanation: Ammonia is known for its pungent smell. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas descriptions.

subtopic: Nitrogen: ammonia: Haber process, properties, uses
question: Ammonia turns damp red litmus paper blue because it is

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why ammonia changes red litmus to blue.
explanation.principle: Ammonia in moisture gives an alkaline solution.
steps: Ammonia dissolves partly in water.
This produces NH₄⁺ and OH⁻ in solution.
The hydroxide ions make the solution alkaline.
Therefore ammonia turns damp red litmus blue because it is alkaline in moist conditions.
explanation.simple_explanation: Ammonia behaves as a basic gas in moisture. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas-litmus tests.

question: When an ammonium salt is warmed with NaOH solution, the gas evolved is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the gas released from an ammonium salt by a strong alkali.
explanation.principle: Strong alkali liberates ammonia from ammonium salts.
steps: The ammonium ion is NH₄⁺.
Heating with NaOH drives off NH₃.
This is the basis of the test for ammonium ions.
Therefore the gas evolved is ammonia.
explanation.simple_explanation: Warm alkali releases NH₃ from ammonium salts. Check our "Tutorial section" for cation tests.

subtopic: Nitrogen: oxidation of ammonia to nitrogen(IV) oxide and trioxonitrate(V) acid
question: In the Ostwald process, the first oxide formed from ammonia is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the first oxide formed during oxidation of ammonia.
explanation.principle: The Ostwald process proceeds stepwise.
steps: Ammonia is first oxidized to nitric oxide, NO.
NO is then oxidized further to NO₂.
These steps lead eventually to nitric acid formation.
Therefore the first oxide formed is NO.
explanation.simple_explanation: The Ostwald process first forms NO. Check our "Tutorial section" for process sequences.

question: A confirmatory test for ammonia evolved from an ammonium salt is that it

options.D: forms a white precipitate with limewater

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a confirmatory test for ammonia gas.
explanation.principle: Ammonia is alkaline in moist conditions.
steps: The gas has a pungent smell.
It also turns moist red litmus blue.
This confirms that the evolved gas is ammonia.
Therefore option B is correct.
explanation.final_answer: B. turns moist red litmus blue
explanation.simple_explanation: Ammonia turns damp red litmus blue. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas tests.

question: Trioxonitrate(V) acid is also known as

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the common name for trioxonitrate(V) acid.
explanation.principle: The systematic name refers to HNO₃.
steps: HNO₃ is called nitric acid.
Its salts are nitrates.
Students should know both the common and systematic names.
Therefore trioxonitrate(V) acid is nitric acid.
explanation.simple_explanation: Nitric acid is trioxonitrate(V) acid. Check our "Tutorial section" for naming practice.

question: A general property of nitrates is that they are

explanation.question_restate: We are to state a common solubility rule about nitrates.
explanation.principle: Nitrates are usually soluble in water.
steps: This is one of the standard solubility rules in chemistry.
It helps explain why nitrates rarely appear as precipitates in simple analysis.
Therefore option B is correct.
Remembering such rules is useful for objective questions.
explanation.simple_explanation: Nitrates are generally soluble in water. Check our "Tutorial section" for solubility rules.

question: Which oxide of nitrogen is brown in colour?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the brown oxide of nitrogen.
explanation.principle: Nitrogen(IV) oxide has a characteristic brown colour.
steps: NO is colourless.
NO₂ is brown.
This feature is often used to identify it in reactions.
Therefore the brown oxide is NO₂.
explanation.simple_explanation: NO₂ is the common brown oxide of nitrogen. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas properties.

question: The colourless oxide that turns brown in air is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a colourless nitrogen oxide that changes in air.
explanation.principle: NO is oxidized by oxygen in the air to brown NO₂.
steps: Fresh NO is colourless.
Air provides oxygen.
This converts NO to NO₂, which is brown.
Therefore the original gas is NO.
explanation.simple_explanation: NO turns into brown NO₂ in air. Check our "Tutorial section" for nitrogen oxides.

question: Nitrogen fixation is important because it converts atmospheric nitrogen into

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the importance of nitrogen fixation.
explanation.principle: Atmospheric N₂ is very stable and not directly usable by many living things.
steps: Fixation changes nitrogen into compounds like ammonia or nitrates.
These forms can be used by plants and living systems.
So fixation makes nitrogen biologically available.
Therefore option A is correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: Nitrogen fixation makes nitrogen usable in the environment. Check our "Tutorial section" for the nitrogen cycle.

question: The conversion of ammonium compounds to nitrates is called

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the process that forms nitrates from ammonium compounds.
explanation.principle: Nitrification is the oxidation stage in the nitrogen cycle.
steps: Ammonium compounds are first converted to nitrites.
They are then converted to nitrates.
The overall process is called nitrification.
Therefore option B is correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: Nitrification converts ammonium compounds into nitrates. Check our "Tutorial section" for cycle terminology.

subtopic: Nitrogen: ammonia: Haber process, properties, uses
question: High pressure is used in the Haber process because the reaction

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why high pressure helps in the Haber process.
explanation.principle: Le Chatelier’s principle says higher pressure favors the side with fewer moles of gas.
steps: The equation is N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃.
There are four moles of gas on the left and two on the right.
High pressure therefore favors ammonia formation.
So the reason is that the reaction produces fewer gas molecules.
explanation.simple_explanation: High pressure favors the side with fewer gas molecules. Check our "Tutorial section" for equilibrium ideas.

subtopic: Nitrogen: ammonia: Haber process, properties, uses
question: Moderate rather than extremely high temperature is used in the Haber process because the reaction is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the reason behind the choice of temperature in the Haber process.
explanation.principle: Exothermic reactions are less favored by very high temperatures at equilibrium.
steps: The Haber reaction releases heat.
Lower temperature favors the equilibrium yield of ammonia.
However, too low a temperature slows the reaction too much.
Therefore a moderate temperature is used because the reaction is exothermic.
explanation.simple_explanation: The Haber process balances equilibrium yield and reaction rate. Check our "Tutorial section" for process conditions.

question: Which of the following is an ammonium salt?

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a salt containing the ammonium ion.
explanation.principle: Ammonium salts contain NH₄⁺ as the positive ion.
steps: NH₄Cl is ammonium chloride.
Its cation is NH₄⁺.
The other compounds contain different cations.
Therefore NH₄Cl is the ammonium salt.
explanation.simple_explanation: Ammonium salts are recognized by NH₄⁺. Check our "Tutorial section" for common ions.

subtopic: Nitrogen: oxidation of ammonia to nitrogen(IV) oxide and trioxonitrate(V) acid
question: The industrial process for converting ammonia to nitric acid is the

explanation.question_restate: We are to name the industrial process used to make nitric acid from ammonia.
explanation.principle: Different major chemicals are produced by different named processes.
steps: The Haber process produces ammonia.
The contact process produces sulphuric acid.
The Ostwald process converts ammonia into nitric acid.
Therefore the correct answer is Ostwald process.
explanation.simple_explanation: Haber makes NH₃, Ostwald makes HNO₃. Check our "Tutorial section" for named processes.

question: The process that returns nitrogen from nitrates in the soil back to the atmosphere is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the process that removes nitrate nitrogen from soil and sends it back to the air.
explanation.principle: Denitrifying bacteria are responsible for this step.
steps: Nitrates are reduced to nitrogen gas or related gaseous compounds.
This returns nitrogen to the atmosphere.
The process reduces the amount of useful nitrate in soil.
Therefore the process is denitrification.
explanation.simple_explanation: Denitrification returns nitrogen to the atmosphere. Check our "Tutorial section" for the nitrogen cycle.

question: Diamond and graphite are allotropes because they

options.B: contain only carbon but differ in structure

answer_text: contain only carbon but differ in structure

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why diamond and graphite are allotropes of carbon.
explanation.principle: Allotropes are different structural forms of the same element.
steps: Both diamond and graphite contain only carbon atoms.
Their arrangements are different.
That structural difference causes different properties.
Therefore they are allotropes.
explanation.final_answer: B. contain only carbon but differ in structure
explanation.simple_explanation: Allotropy means same element, different structure. Check our "Tutorial section" for structure-property links.

question: Diamond is used for cutting glass because it is

explanation.question_restate: We are to relate a property of diamond to its use.
explanation.principle: The giant covalent structure of diamond makes it extremely hard.
steps: Strong covalent bonds hold the structure rigidly.
This gives diamond exceptional hardness.
A very hard material can scratch and cut glass.
Therefore diamond is used because it is extremely hard.
explanation.simple_explanation: Diamond cuts glass because of its extreme hardness. Check our "Tutorial section" for allotrope uses.

question: Graphite conducts electricity because it has

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why graphite conducts electricity.
explanation.principle: Conduction in graphite is due to mobile delocalized electrons.
steps: Each carbon atom in graphite contributes one electron to a delocalized system.
These electrons move through the layers.
That movement carries charge.
Therefore graphite conducts because of delocalized electrons.
explanation.simple_explanation: Graphite conducts because it contains delocalized electrons. Check our "Tutorial section" for giant structures.

question: When carbon burns completely in excess oxygen, the product is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the product of complete combustion of carbon.
explanation.principle: Complete combustion means there is enough oxygen for full oxidation.
steps: Carbon reacts with oxygen during burning.
With enough oxygen, the product is carbon(IV) oxide.
Incomplete combustion would produce carbon(II) oxide instead.
Therefore the product is CO₂.
explanation.simple_explanation: Complete combustion of carbon gives CO₂. Check our "Tutorial section" for combustion products.

question: Carbon(IV) oxide turns limewater milky because it produces

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the limewater test for carbon(IV) oxide.
explanation.principle: The milkiness is due to a white insoluble precipitate.
steps: Limewater is calcium hydroxide solution.
CO₂ reacts with it to form calcium carbonate and water.
Calcium carbonate is white and insoluble.
Therefore CaCO₃ causes the milkiness.
explanation.simple_explanation: The limewater test gives a CaCO₃ precipitate. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas tests.

question: Carbon(II) oxide is formed during combustion when oxygen supply is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the condition that leads to the formation of carbon(II) oxide.
explanation.principle: Limited oxygen leads to incomplete combustion.
steps: If oxygen is abundant, carbon burns to CO₂.
If oxygen is limited, incomplete combustion occurs.
One major product can then be CO.
Therefore limited oxygen is the correct condition.
explanation.simple_explanation: CO is associated with incomplete combustion. Check our "Tutorial section" for combustion conditions.

question: Carbon(II) oxide is dangerous because it

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why carbon(II) oxide is poisonous.
explanation.principle: CO interferes with oxygen transport in the blood.
steps: Haemoglobin normally carries oxygen.
CO binds very strongly to haemoglobin.
This prevents normal oxygen transport to body tissues.
Therefore CO is dangerous because it combines strongly with haemoglobin.
explanation.simple_explanation: CO poisoning occurs because oxygen transport is blocked. Check our "Tutorial section" for safety notes.

question: Coal is best described as

options.B: a naturally occurring carbonaceous substance containing impurities

answer_text: a naturally occurring carbonaceous substance containing impurities

explanation.question_restate: We are to describe coal correctly.
explanation.principle: Coal is rich in carbon but is not chemically pure.
steps: It contains carbon together with hydrogen, sulphur, moisture and mineral matter.
Its composition varies depending on the type of coal.
Therefore it is not pure carbon.
So it is a naturally occurring carbonaceous substance containing impurities.
explanation.final_answer: B. a naturally occurring carbonaceous substance containing impurities
explanation.simple_explanation: Coal is carbon-rich but not pure. Check our "Tutorial section" for fossil fuel chemistry.

question: Coke is the

options.B: carbon-rich solid residue from destructive distillation of coal
options.C: gas from fractional distillation of air

answer_text: carbon-rich solid residue from destructive distillation of coal

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify what coke is.
explanation.principle: Coke is the solid product left after volatile substances are driven off from coal.
steps: Coal is heated strongly without air.
Volatile materials leave the coal.
A carbon-rich porous solid remains.
That solid is coke.
explanation.final_answer: B. carbon-rich solid residue from destructive distillation of coal
explanation.simple_explanation: Coke is the solid residue from coal distillation. Check our "Tutorial section" for coal products.

subtopic: Carbon: destructive distillation of wood and coal
question: Destructive distillation of coal produces

options.A: coke, coal tar and coal gas

answer_text: coke, coal tar and coal gas

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify products from destructive distillation of coal.
explanation.principle: Heating coal strongly without air produces solid, liquid and gaseous products.
steps: Coke is the solid residue.
Coal tar is a liquid fraction.
Coal gas is gaseous.
Therefore the products include coke, coal tar and coal gas.
explanation.final_answer: A. coke, coal tar and coal gas
explanation.simple_explanation: These are standard products from destructive distillation of coal. Check our "Tutorial section" for product lists.

question: Synthetic gas is mainly made up of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the composition of synthetic gas.
explanation.principle: Syngas is an industrial gas mixture used as a feedstock.
steps: The two main components are carbon(II) oxide and hydrogen.
This mixture can be used to make other important chemicals.
That is why it is called synthesis gas.
Therefore it is mainly CO and H₂.
explanation.simple_explanation: Syngas mainly contains CO and H₂. Check our "Tutorial section" for industrial gases.

question: A major use of synthetic gas is the manufacture of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a common industrial use of syngas.
explanation.principle: Syngas is a feedstock for methanol production and other syntheses.
steps: CO and H₂ can be reacted under suitable conditions.
Methanol is one important product made this way.
This makes syngas valuable industrially.
Therefore methanol is the correct answer.
explanation.simple_explanation: Syngas is a major feedstock for methanol. Check our "Tutorial section" for industrial applications.

question: If excess CO₂ is passed through limewater after the initial milkiness appears, the milkiness disappears because

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why limewater clears again in excess carbon(IV) oxide.
explanation.principle: The first precipitate can dissolve on further reaction.
steps: Initially, CO₂ forms insoluble CaCO₃ with limewater.
With more CO₂, the CaCO₃ reacts further with water and CO₂.
A soluble hydrogencarbonate is formed.
Therefore the milkiness disappears because soluble calcium hydrogencarbonate forms.
explanation.simple_explanation: Excess CO₂ can dissolve the first precipitate. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas-test exceptions.

question: Which statement about carbon(II) oxide is correct?

options.A: it is acidic and turns limewater milky directly
options.B: it is neutral and acts as a reducing agent
options.D: it is highly soluble and basic

answer_text: it is neutral and acts as a reducing agent

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a correct description of carbon(II) oxide.
explanation.principle: CO is a neutral oxide but has important reducing properties.
steps: It does not behave like an acidic oxide in the usual sense.
It can reduce some metallic oxides.
This makes it useful in metallurgy.
Therefore it is neutral and acts as a reducing agent.
explanation.final_answer: B. it is neutral and acts as a reducing agent
explanation.simple_explanation: CO is neutral as an oxide but useful in reduction reactions. Check our "Tutorial section" for oxide classes.

question: Graphite is used as a lubricant because

options.A: its layers slide easily over one another
options.B: it is as hard as diamond

answer_text: its layers slide easily over one another

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why graphite can serve as a lubricant.
explanation.principle: The weak forces between graphite layers allow them to move easily.
steps: Graphite has a layered structure.
The layers are held together by weak forces.
This allows them to slide over each other.
Therefore graphite acts as a lubricant.
explanation.final_answer: A. its layers slide easily over one another
explanation.simple_explanation: Graphite is slippery because its layers can slide. Check our "Tutorial section" for allotrope properties.

question: As coal changes from peat to anthracite, the carbon content generally

explanation.question_restate: We are to describe the general trend in the composition of coal types.
explanation.principle: More mature coal types are richer in carbon.
steps: Peat has relatively low carbon content.
Anthracite is richer in carbon and has less moisture and volatile matter.
This shows an increase in carbon content through coalification.
Therefore the carbon content generally increases.
explanation.simple_explanation: Carbon content generally rises as coal matures. Check our "Tutorial section" for coal types.

question: A major use of coke in the blast furnace is as

explanation.question_restate: We are to state an important use of coke in metallurgy.
explanation.principle: Coke helps provide heat and reducing conditions in extraction furnaces.
steps: Coke burns to provide heat.
It also helps form carbon(II) oxide, which reduces iron oxides.
This makes coke valuable in the blast furnace.
Therefore it is used as a reducing agent and fuel.
explanation.final_answer: A. a reducing agent and fuel
explanation.simple_explanation: Coke supplies both heat and reduction in metallurgy. Check our "Tutorial section" for extraction chemistry.

subtopic: Carbon: destructive distillation of wood and coal
question: Which product is more closely associated with destructive distillation of wood than with coal?

explanation.question_restate: We are to distinguish between products associated with wood and with coal.
explanation.principle: The names of the products often reflect the original material.
steps: Wood heated without air gives wood charcoal and related products.
Coal heated without air gives coke, coal tar and coal gas.
So wood charcoal is associated with wood rather than coal.
Therefore option A is correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: Keep wood products separate from coal products in revision. Check our "Tutorial section" for comparison tables.

question: Synthetic gas is called a feedstock because it

options.B: serves as a starting material for other chemical syntheses

answer_text: serves as a starting material for other chemical syntheses

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why syngas is called a feedstock.
explanation.principle: A feedstock is a raw material used to make other useful products.
steps: Syngas contains CO and H₂.
These gases can be converted into methanol and other chemicals.
So the mixture is used as a starting material rather than only as a final product.
Therefore syngas is called a feedstock because it serves in other syntheses.
explanation.final_answer: B. serves as a starting material for other chemical syntheses
explanation.simple_explanation: Syngas is valued because it can be turned into many useful products. Check our "Tutorial section" for industrial chemistry.

question: Which statement best compares carbon(IV) oxide with carbon(II) oxide?

options.A: CO₂ is an acidic oxide, while CO is a neutral oxide and reducing agent
options.B: CO₂ is a reducing gas, while CO is acidic
options.C: both are brown gases and both turn limewater milky directly
options.D: CO₂ is poisonous because it combines strongly with haemoglobin, while CO is harmless

answer_text: CO₂ is an acidic oxide, while CO is a neutral oxide and reducing agent

explanation.question_restate: We are to compare the two common oxides of carbon.
explanation.principle: Carbon(IV) oxide and carbon(II) oxide differ clearly in both classification and behavior.
steps: CO₂ is usually classified as an acidic oxide because it forms an acid in water and reacts with bases.
CO is usually classified as a neutral oxide.
CO can also act as a reducing agent in metallurgical processes.
Therefore the best comparison is that CO₂ is acidic, while CO is neutral and reducing.
explanation.final_answer: A. CO₂ is an acidic oxide, while CO is a neutral oxide and reducing agent
explanation.simple_explanation: This comparison is very common in exam questions on carbon compounds. Check our "Tutorial section" for side-by-side revision tables.

subtopic: Hydrogen: commercial production from water gas and petroleum cracking
question: The reaction between steam and red-hot carbon is important in hydrogen manufacture because it produces

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the gas mixture formed when steam reacts with red-hot carbon.
explanation.principle: The products CO and H₂ together are called water gas.
steps: Steam reacts with red-hot coke or carbon.
The main gaseous products are CO and H₂.
This mixture is important as a starting point for hydrogen production.
Therefore the product is water gas.
explanation.simple_explanation: Steam on red-hot carbon gives water gas. Check our "Tutorial section" for industrial gas mixtures.

subtopic: Hydrogen: commercial production from water gas and petroleum cracking
question: In the water-gas shift reaction, the role of steam is to react with

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why steam is used after water gas has been formed.
explanation.principle: Steam reacts with carbon(II) oxide to increase hydrogen production.
steps: Water gas contains CO and H₂.
The equation CO + H₂O ⇌ CO₂ + H₂ shows how steam is used.
This creates extra hydrogen and carbon(IV) oxide.
Therefore steam reacts with CO to produce more H₂.
explanation.final_answer: A. CO to produce more H₂
explanation.simple_explanation: The shift reaction increases the hydrogen content of the gas mixture. Check our "Tutorial section" for industrial preparation.

subtopic: Hydrogen: commercial production from water gas and petroleum cracking
question: Hydrogen can be produced from petroleum mainly through

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify petroleum-based processes linked to hydrogen production.
explanation.principle: Hydrocarbon feedstocks can be broken down and further processed to yield hydrogen-rich mixtures.
steps: Petroleum fractions are made of hydrocarbons.
Cracking and reforming change these into smaller molecules.
These products can undergo reactions such as steam reforming.
Therefore cracking and reforming are important.
explanation.simple_explanation: Hydrogen often comes from processed hydrocarbon feedstocks. Check our "Tutorial section" for industrial chemistry.

question: A common metal used in the laboratory preparation of hydrogen is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a suitable metal for preparing hydrogen from dilute acid.
explanation.principle: A suitable metal should be above hydrogen in the reactivity series.
steps: Zinc is reactive enough to displace hydrogen from dilute acids.
It reacts with dilute HCl or dilute H₂SO₄ to release H₂.
Copper, silver and gold are not suitable in the ordinary method.
Therefore zinc is commonly used.
explanation.simple_explanation: Zinc is commonly used because it reacts with dilute acids to give H₂. Check our "Tutorial section" for reactivity series.

question: In preparing hydrogen with zinc and dilute acid, the first gas is allowed to escape mainly because it may contain

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain a safety precaution in hydrogen preparation.
explanation.principle: At the beginning, the apparatus contains air before hydrogen displaces it.
steps: The flask and delivery tube are initially full of air.
As hydrogen forms, it mixes with the air present.
This can make an explosive mixture.
Therefore the first gas is allowed to escape because it may contain air.
explanation.simple_explanation: Discard the first portion of gas because it may be mixed with air. Check our "Tutorial section" for laboratory safety.

question: Which acid is often preferred to nitric acid in preparing hydrogen from a metal because nitric acid

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why nitric acid is often not used in the ordinary preparation of hydrogen.
explanation.principle: Nitric acid frequently behaves as an oxidizing acid and may not liberate hydrogen normally.
steps: Reactive metals usually give hydrogen with dilute non-oxidizing acids.
Nitric acid often forms oxides of nitrogen instead.
So it is usually avoided for the normal hydrogen preparation.
Therefore the reason is that nitric acid is oxidizing.
explanation.simple_explanation: Nitric acid often behaves differently because it is oxidizing. Check our "Tutorial section" for acid-choice rules.

question: Which statement about hydrogen is correct?

options.D: It turns moist blue litmus red

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose a correct general property of hydrogen.
explanation.principle: Hydrogen is a simple gas with no colour and no smell.
steps: Hydrogen has no colour.
It also has no odour.
It is not an oxide and does not show acidic gas behavior.
Therefore the correct statement is that it is odourless and colourless.
explanation.final_answer: B. It is odourless and colourless
explanation.simple_explanation: Hydrogen is a colourless, odourless gas. Check our "Tutorial section" for quick property tables.

question: Hydrogen acts as a reducing agent when it is passed over hot copper(II) oxide because it

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain what hydrogen does when it reacts with hot CuO.
explanation.principle: A reducing agent removes oxygen from another substance.
steps: Copper(II) oxide is black.
Hydrogen takes oxygen away from it and forms water.
Copper metal is left behind.
Therefore hydrogen removes oxygen from CuO.
explanation.simple_explanation: Hydrogen reduces CuO by taking away oxygen. Check our "Tutorial section" for redox examples.

question: A major industrial use of hydrogen is in the manufacture of

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify an important industrial use of hydrogen.
explanation.principle: Hydrogen is used with nitrogen in the Haber process.
steps: Ammonia has the formula NH₃.
It is made by combining nitrogen and hydrogen.
This is one of the largest industrial uses of hydrogen.
Therefore NH₃ is correct.
explanation.simple_explanation: Hydrogen is important in ammonia manufacture. Check our "Tutorial section" for the Haber process.

question: Hydrogen is used in the hardening of oils because it

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain how hydrogen hardens unsaturated oils.
explanation.principle: Unsaturated oils contain carbon-carbon double bonds that can react by addition.
steps: The oil molecules contain C=C bonds.
Hydrogen adds across these bonds in the presence of a catalyst.
This makes the oil more saturated and harder.
Therefore hydrogen hardens oils by adding across double bonds.
explanation.simple_explanation: Hydrogenation is an addition reaction across C=C bonds. Check our "Tutorial section" for organic applications.

question: The low density of hydrogen makes it useful for

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a use based on hydrogen’s low density.
explanation.principle: A gas less dense than air can provide lift.
steps: Hydrogen is very light.
A balloon filled with hydrogen experiences upthrust in air.
This makes it rise.
Therefore hydrogen can be used in balloons.
explanation.simple_explanation: Hydrogen is light enough to provide lift in balloons. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas uses.

question: Although hydrogen gives good lift in balloons, helium is often preferred because helium is

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why helium is often chosen instead of hydrogen in practical balloon use.
explanation.principle: Safety is a major practical consideration when choosing a gas.
steps: Hydrogen is flammable.
Helium is chemically inert and does not burn.
This makes helium safer in balloons.
Therefore helium is preferred because it is non-flammable.
explanation.simple_explanation: Safety often determines which gas is preferred in real life. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas comparisons.

question: Hydrogen is identified in the laboratory because it

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the standard laboratory test for hydrogen.
explanation.principle: Hydrogen ignites rapidly and gives a characteristic sound.
steps: A lighted splint is brought near the gas.
The gas burns quickly with oxygen from the air.
This produces a pop sound.
Therefore hydrogen is identified by the squeaky pop.
explanation.final_answer: B. burns with a squeaky pop
explanation.simple_explanation: The pop test is the classic test for hydrogen. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas tests.

question: The squeaky pop heard during the hydrogen test is caused by

options.B: rapid combustion of H₂ with air

answer_text: rapid combustion of H₂ with air

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the source of the pop sound in the hydrogen test.
explanation.principle: The sound is due to fast burning of hydrogen in air.
steps: Hydrogen is combustible.
When ignited, it reacts rapidly with oxygen from the air.
This sudden combustion produces the pop sound.
Therefore the sound is due to rapid combustion of H₂ with air.
explanation.final_answer: B. rapid combustion of H₂ with air
explanation.simple_explanation: The pop comes from fast burning of hydrogen with oxygen. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas-test explanations.

question: Hydrogen is collected over water mainly because it is

options.B: only slightly soluble and does not react with water during collection

answer_text: only slightly soluble and does not react with water during collection

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why hydrogen can be collected over water.
explanation.principle: A gas collected over water should not dissolve much or react appreciably with water.
steps: Hydrogen is only slightly soluble in water.
It does not react significantly with water under collection conditions.
So it can displace water and be collected in an inverted container.
Therefore option B is correct.
explanation.final_answer: B. only slightly soluble and does not react with water during collection
explanation.simple_explanation: Hydrogen can be collected over water because little of it dissolves. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas collection methods.

question: Hydrogen burns in oxygen to produce

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the product formed when hydrogen burns in oxygen.
explanation.principle: Combustion of hydrogen produces water.
steps: The balanced equation is 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O.
The product may appear as steam or condensed droplets.
No carbon is present, so carbon oxides are not formed.
Therefore the product is H₂O.
explanation.simple_explanation: Burning hydrogen produces water. Check our "Tutorial section" for combustion equations.

subtopic: Hydrogen: commercial production from water gas and petroleum cracking
question: After the water-gas shift reaction, a common gas removed to obtain purer hydrogen is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a gas commonly removed after the shift reaction.
explanation.principle: The shift reaction produces carbon(IV) oxide alongside hydrogen.
steps: The equation produces CO₂ and H₂.
Hydrogen is the desired product.
CO₂ is removed to purify the hydrogen stream.
Therefore CO₂ is the gas commonly removed.
explanation.simple_explanation: Removing CO₂ helps purify hydrogen after the shift reaction. Check our "Tutorial section" for industrial gas purification.

question: Which equation correctly represents the reaction between magnesium and hydrochloric acid?

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose the balanced equation for magnesium reacting with hydrochloric acid.
explanation.principle: Magnesium forms Mg²⁺ and hydrogen ions combine to form H₂.
steps: Magnesium forms magnesium chloride, MgCl₂.
Two HCl molecules provide the necessary two chloride ions and the hydrogen ions.
The hydrogen ions combine to form H₂ gas.
Therefore the correct equation is Mg + 2HCl → MgCl₂ + H₂.
explanation.simple_explanation: Balanced equations help show clearly how salt and gas are formed. Check our "Tutorial section" for worked equations.

question: Hydrogen diffuses quickly mainly because its molecules are

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why hydrogen diffuses rapidly.
explanation.principle: Lighter gas particles move more quickly on average at the same temperature.
steps: Hydrogen molecules have very low mass.
This allows the gas to spread rapidly through space.
That is why hydrogen diffuses faster than many heavier gases.
Therefore the molecules are very light.
explanation.simple_explanation: Hydrogen diffuses rapidly because its molecules are very light. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas behavior.

question: A safety reason for keeping the mouth of the test tube away from the face during the hydrogen test is that hydrogen

options.A: may ignite suddenly with a pop

answer_text: may ignite suddenly with a pop

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain a safety rule used during the hydrogen test.
explanation.principle: Hydrogen can ignite suddenly when brought into contact with a flame.
steps: Near the mouth of the tube, hydrogen mixes with air.
When a lighted splint is applied, the gas may burn rapidly.
The pop can occur suddenly.
Therefore the tube is kept away from the face because hydrogen may ignite suddenly.
explanation.final_answer: A. may ignite suddenly with a pop
explanation.simple_explanation: Safety matters because hydrogen can ignite quickly. Check our "Tutorial section" for laboratory precautions.

question: Chlorine is a representative halogen because it has

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the feature that makes chlorine a halogen.
explanation.principle: Halogens are Group 17 elements with seven valence electrons.
steps: Chlorine has seven electrons in its outer shell.
This is typical of the halogen family.
Such elements tend to gain one electron in simple ionic compounds.

explanation.simple_explanation: Group 17 elements have seven valence electrons. Check our "Tutorial section" for periodic properties.

question: A typical halogen behavior shown by chlorine is that it

explanation.question_restate: We are to choose the behavior that shows chlorine acting like a halogen.

steps: Chlorine needs one more electron to complete its octet.

This is typical of Group 17 chemistry.
Therefore chlorine forms Cl⁻ by gaining one electron.
explanation.final_answer: A. forms Cl⁻ by gaining one electron
explanation.simple_explanation: Chlorine acts like a typical halogen when it forms chloride ions. Check our "Tutorial section" for group trends.

question: The common laboratory preparation of chlorine uses concentrated HCl and

explanation.simple_explanation: Chlorine is commonly prepared in the lab using MnO₂ and concentrated HCl. Check our "Tutorial section" for preparations.

question: Chlorine is usually collected by downward delivery because it is

options.B: denser than air and fairly soluble in water

answer_text: denser than air and fairly soluble in water

explanation.principle: Collection depends on density and solubility.
steps: Chlorine is denser than air.

explanation.final_answer: B. denser than air and fairly soluble in water
explanation.simple_explanation: Chlorine is collected by downward delivery because water is unsuitable. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas collection.

question: The industrial source of chlorine is mainly

explanation.principle: Brine is concentrated sodium chloride solution used in the chlor-alkali process.
steps: Brine is passed through an electrolytic cell.

question: During electrolysis of brine, the ion discharged at the anode is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the ion responsible for chlorine formation in brine electrolysis.

question: One important use of chlorine is in

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify a common practical use of chlorine.
explanation.principle: Chlorine is used to kill harmful microorganisms in water.
steps: Small quantities of chlorine or chlorine compounds are added to water.

This helps make water safe for use.
Therefore one important use is disinfecting water.
explanation.simple_explanation: Chlorine is widely used in water treatment. Check our "Tutorial section" for applications.

question: Chlorine bleaches mainly because in moist conditions it acts as

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain the bleaching action of chlorine.
explanation.principle: Moist chlorine produces species that oxidize coloured substances.
steps: Dry chlorine shows little bleaching effect.

These oxidize the coloured material to colourless products.
Therefore chlorine bleaches mainly by acting as an oxidizing agent.
explanation.simple_explanation: Chlorine bleaches by oxidation in the presence of moisture. Check our "Tutorial section" for bleaching agents.

subtopic: Halogens: hydrogen chloride and hydrochloric acid
question: Hydrochloric acid is formed when hydrogen chloride gas

explanation.question_restate: We are to distinguish hydrogen chloride gas from hydrochloric acid.

steps: HCl gas in the dry state is simply hydrogen chloride.

subtopic: Halogens: hydrogen chloride and hydrochloric acid
question: Dry HCl gas does not change dry blue litmus because

explanation.principle: Indicator reactions usually depend on ions in water.
steps: Dry HCl is covalent.
Without water, the ions responsible for acidity are not produced in the usual way.
So dry blue litmus does not turn red.
Therefore the reason is that it does not ionize without water.
explanation.final_answer: B. it does not ionize without water
explanation.simple_explanation: The usual litmus change requires water. Check our "Tutorial section" for acid behavior.

subtopic: Halogens: chlorides and test for chlorides
question: Chloride ions are tested for using acidified

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the reagent used to test for chloride ions.

steps: The solution is first acidified with dilute nitric acid.

A white precipitate of AgCl indicates chloride ions.
Therefore acidified AgNO₃ is the correct reagent.
explanation.simple_explanation: Acidified silver nitrate is the standard chloride test. Check our "Tutorial section" for anion tests.

subtopic: Halogens: chlorides and test for chlorides
question: The precipitate produced in the chloride test is

explanation.question_restate: We are to identify the precipitate formed when silver nitrate reacts with chloride ions.
explanation.principle: Silver chloride is the insoluble product.
steps: Silver ions come from AgNO₃.

explanation.simple_explanation: Colour and smell are useful clues in identifying chlorine. Check our "Tutorial section" for gas summaries.

explanation.simple_explanation: Moisture activates chlorine bleaching. Check our "Tutorial section" for the underlying chemistry.

subtopic: Halogens: hydrogen chloride and hydrochloric acid

subtopic: Halogens: chlorides and test for chlorides
question: Why is dilute nitric acid used before AgNO₃ in the chloride test instead of dilute HCl?

question: Chlorine water loses colour on standing in sunlight mainly because

answer_text: chlorine is destroyed in light-driven reactions

explanation.question_restate: We are to explain why chlorine water fades in sunlight.
explanation.principle: Chlorine water is unstable in light and the dissolved chlorine is gradually lost through reactions.
steps: The dissolved chlorine and related species undergo photochemical changes.

As a result, the colour becomes less intense.
Therefore chlorine water fades because chlorine is destroyed in light-driven reactions.
explanation.final_answer: A. chlorine is destroyed in light-driven reactions
explanation.simple_explanation: Chlorine water is unstable in light, so its colour fades. Check our "Tutorial section" for common observations.

subtopic: Oxygen and sulphur: ozone as an allotrope and its atmospheric importance
